stem: The support agent receives an ambiguous refund request it cannot confidently resolve within policy. What is the most appropriate orchestration decision?
options: Guess a resolution to preserve the first-contact-resolution metric
Call escalate_to_human with the gathered context when confidence or policy coverage is insufficient
Loop indefinitely calling lookup_order until certain
Refuse to respond
rationale: A well-designed agent knows its limits: when confidence or policy coverage is insufficient it should escalate to a human with the context it has gathered, rather than guess (harming trust) or loop.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Agentic Architecture & Orchestration

stem: Which is the best design for the process_refund MCP tool given it moves money?
options: Make it fire-and-forget with no confirmation
Give it a precise input schema, require explicit parameters (order id, amount), and design it to be idempotent/confirmable so retries don't double-refund
Let the model pass free-form text and parse it server-side
Combine refund and escalation into one ambiguous tool
rationale: Money-moving tools need a precise input_schema, explicit required parameters, and idempotency so retries or duplicate tool calls do not cause double refunds. Free-form input and merged responsibilities invite errors.
referenceText: Tool use — defining tools

stem: To keep the agent reliable across a long multi-turn dispute, which context-management practice helps most?
options: Send the entire raw chat and all tool outputs every turn with no structure
Maintain concise, structured state (verified facts, order status, decisions) so key context persists without unbounded growth
Clear all context each turn to save tokens
Rely on the model to remember across separate API calls with no history
rationale: Reliability comes from managing context deliberately: carry concise structured state forward rather than dumping everything (which risks context bloat and distraction) or discarding needed context.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Context Management & Reliability

stem: For a large, risky refactor across many files, which Claude Code approach is most appropriate before making changes?
options: Direct execution immediately without review
Use plan mode to produce and review a plan before Claude edits files
Delete the files and regenerate from scratch
Disable CLAUDE.md so there are no constraints
rationale: Plan mode lets Claude Code propose a plan you review before it edits, which is appropriate for large/risky changes. Direct execution suits small, low-risk tasks.
referenceText: Claude Code — plan mode & workflows

stem: Which belong in a well-designed CLAUDE.md for the team's repository? (Select TWO.)
options: Project conventions, build/test commands, and architectural notes
Reusable guidance Claude should apply consistently across the repo
Secrets such as API keys and tokens
A copy of the entire dependency source tree
rationale: CLAUDE.md should capture conventions, commands, and durable guidance Claude applies consistently. Secrets must never be committed, and vendoring all dependency source into it is inappropriate.
referenceText: Claude Code — CLAUDE.md / memory

stem: During a long Claude Code session the context is filling with irrelevant history, degrading focus. What is the best reliability practice?
options: Keep appending everything indefinitely
Curate context — clear or summarize stale content and provide only the files/scope relevant to the current task
Randomly truncate the oldest half of the context
Increase temperature to compensate
rationale: Curating context (clearing/summarizing stale content, scoping to relevant files) preserves reliability. Indefinite growth and blind truncation both harm results.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Context Management & Reliability

stem: In the coordinator/subagent research system, what is the coordinator's primary architectural responsibility?
options: Doing all searching, analysis, and writing itself
Decomposing the task, delegating to specialized subagents, and synthesizing their results
Storing the final report in a database
Replacing the need for any subagents
rationale: In an orchestrator/worker pattern the coordinator decomposes the task, delegates to specialized subagents, and integrates their outputs — the core of multi-agent orchestration.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Agentic Architecture & Orchestration

stem: A search subagent fails on some queries. What should it return so the coordinator can proceed reliably?
options: A generic "error" string with no detail
Structured error context: failure type, what was attempted, any partial results, and alternative approaches
Nothing, and stop the whole run
A hallucinated result to avoid reporting failure
rationale: Returning structured error context (failure type, attempt, partial results, alternatives) lets the coordinator proceed and annotate coverage gaps. Generic errors, silent failure, or fabrication all reduce reliability.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Context Management & Reliability

stem: The document-analysis subagent exposes tools to the coordinator. Which tool-design choice best supports composition?
options: One giant tool that does search, analysis, and formatting together
Focused tools with clear names, precise input schemas, and structured outputs the coordinator can consume
Tools that return unstructured prose only
Tools with no descriptions to keep the schema small
rationale: Composable multi-agent systems rely on focused, well-described tools with precise schemas and structured outputs. Overloaded or undocumented tools are hard for the model to use correctly.
referenceText: Tool use — tool design

stem: To help engineers explore an unfamiliar codebase, which built-in Claude Code tools are most directly useful?
options: Read, Grep, and Glob to locate and read relevant files
Only Bash to run the app
A web browser tool only
None; the model should guess file contents
rationale: Read, Grep, and Glob let the agent locate and read the right code, which is the foundation of codebase exploration. Guessing or relying solely on running the app is unreliable.
referenceText: Claude Code — built-in tools

stem: The productivity agent must reach an internal ticketing system. What is the appropriate integration mechanism?
options: Hardcode credentials into the prompt
Expose the ticketing system through an MCP server providing well-scoped tools
Ask the user to paste ticket data every time
Fine-tune the model on the ticket database
rationale: An MCP server is the standard way to connect an agent to an external system through well-scoped tools/resources. Hardcoding secrets or manual pasting are poor designs, and fine-tuning is unnecessary here.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol

stem: For automating a repetitive multi-step task, which agent design is most robust?
options: A single prompt that must get everything right at once
An agent loop that plans, calls tools, checks results, and iterates until the task's success criteria are met
Running all steps blindly in parallel with no verification
Hardcoding outputs for every possible input
rationale: A verifying agent loop (plan → act → check → iterate against success criteria) is robust to variation. Single-shot prompts and unverified parallelism are brittle.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Agentic Architecture & Orchestration

stem: The CI code-review bot produces too many false-positive comments. Which prompt-engineering change best reduces them?
options: Tell it to comment on everything it notices
Give explicit criteria for what warrants a comment, ask it to report only high-confidence, actionable issues, and provide few-shot examples of good vs noise
Raise temperature so it varies its output
Remove all instructions and let it decide
rationale: Defining clear criteria, requiring high-confidence actionable findings, and providing few-shot examples of signal vs noise directly reduces false positives. Broad instructions or higher temperature increase noise.
referenceText: Prompt engineering — be clear and use examples

stem: How is Claude Code best invoked inside a non-interactive CI/CD pipeline?
options: It cannot run in CI
In headless/non-interactive mode driven by a command and prompt, with output consumed by the pipeline
Only by a human typing into a terminal
By committing prompts to the repo and waiting
rationale: Claude Code supports headless/non-interactive execution suitable for CI, where it runs from a command/prompt and the pipeline consumes its output. It is not limited to interactive terminals.
referenceText: Claude Code — CI / headless usage

stem: To make generated test cases trustworthy, the prompt should ask Claude to:
options: Generate as many tests as possible regardless of relevance
Ground tests in the actual code/spec, cover meaningful cases and edge cases, and avoid duplicating existing tests
Only test the happy path
Assert that all tests pass without running them
rationale: Trustworthy test generation is grounded in the real code/spec, targets meaningful and edge cases, and avoids duplicates — not sheer volume or happy-path-only coverage.
referenceText: Testing & evaluation guidance

stem: To guarantee extracted records match a required JSON structure, the most reliable design is to:
options: Ask for JSON in prose and regex-parse the reply
Define a tool with a JSON input_schema and use tool use so outputs conform to the schema, then validate against it
Lower max_tokens until only JSON fits
Trust the model to always emit valid JSON
rationale: Structured output is most reliable when the schema is enforced via tool use and then validated. Prose-plus-regex and truncation are brittle, and blind trust fails on edge cases.
referenceText: Tool use for structured output

stem: Some source documents are messy and occasionally missing fields. Which approach best maintains accuracy and downstream reliability?
options: Force the model to invent values for missing fields
Have the model output field-level confidence and mark missing/uncertain fields for review rather than fabricating
Reject any document that isn't perfect
Ignore validation to keep throughput high
rationale: Emitting field-level confidence and flagging missing/uncertain values for review preserves accuracy and enables safe downstream use. Fabricating values or skipping validation harms reliability.
referenceText: CCAR-F guide — Structured Output & Reliability

stem: Which prompt-engineering technique most improves extraction consistency on ambiguous formats?
options: Zero instructions
A few targeted few-shot examples that demonstrate the desired parsing and output format
Higher temperature
A longer max_tokens only
rationale: Few-shot examples that demonstrate the exact parsing and output format are a documented, effective way to improve consistency on ambiguous inputs.
referenceText: Multishot (few-shot) prompting

stem: A customer opens the chat with "This is ridiculous, I want my money back for the thing I ordered." The agent has get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, and escalate_to_human. What is the best-designed first action?
options: Immediately call process_refund with the agent's best guess at the order and amount to maximize first-contact resolution.
Call get_customer and lookup_order to gather order context, then ask one targeted clarifying question if the correct order is still ambiguous.
Call escalate_to_human, because any refund request is outside the agent's authority.
Reply asking the customer to restate the request more politely before taking any action.
rationale: The request is high-ambiguity: which order and what resolution are unknown. Gathering context with read tools and then disambiguating is the reliable pattern. Option 0 risks refunding the wrong order (guessing on action tools is unsafe). Option 2 needlessly escalates a routine task and hurts the 80% first-contact target. Option 3 is a non-action that stalls resolution and mishandles an upset customer.
referenceText: Give Claude the tools to retrieve context first, and design workflows so it acts on grounded information rather than guessing when inputs are ambiguous.

stem: process_refund returns a structured error saying the refund amount exceeds the agent's authorized limit for this account tier. What is the best next step?
options: Retry process_refund repeatedly until it eventually succeeds.
Split the refund into several smaller process_refund calls that each stay under the limit.
Call escalate_to_human with the order details and the reason the automated refund could not complete.
Tell the customer the refund is impossible and close the conversation.
rationale: The error is a policy boundary, not a transient failure, so escalation with full context is the correct, honest handoff. Option 0 will never succeed and wastes calls. Option 1 deliberately circumvents an authorization control. Option 3 abandons a customer who may legitimately be owed the refund. Escalation with context preserves resolution quality.
referenceText: When a task exceeds the agent's authority or a guardrail blocks completion, hand off to a human with the relevant context rather than working around the limit.

stem: You are defining the input_schema for process_refund so Claude reliably supplies correct arguments. Which two design choices most improve reliable tool calls? (Select 2.)
options: Mark order_id and amount as required, and describe each field's meaning and units in the schema.
Use an enum for reason_code to constrain it to the supported set of values.
Leave amount as a free-form string so the model can include currency symbols and notes.
Omit all field descriptions to keep the schema small and save tokens.
Accept the entire raw customer message as one opaque text field and parse it server-side.
rationale: Clear required fields with descriptions and constrained enums are the core JSON-schema practices that make tool arguments reliable and machine-checkable. Option 2 invites malformed amounts. Option 3 removes the descriptions Claude relies on to fill fields correctly. Option 4 pushes ambiguity into the server and defeats the purpose of a typed schema.
referenceText: Provide detailed descriptions and well-specified JSON schemas — required fields, types, and enums — so the model supplies well-formed arguments.

stem: Where should the durable operating rules for the support agent — its role, escalation policy, refund limits, and tone — live so they apply on every run of the Agent SDK app?
options: In the system prompt / CLAUDE.md-style project memory loaded at the start of each session.
Appended as a suffix to the end of every individual customer message.
Encoded only inside each MCP tool's description field.
Stored in a separate database that the model happens to query mid-conversation.
rationale: Persistent role, policy, and tone belong in the system prompt / CLAUDE.md memory that loads every session, giving consistent behavior. Option 1 is fragile and pollutes every user turn. Option 2 overloads tool descriptions with policy they should not carry. Option 3 is not guaranteed to be in context when decisions are made.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md and system-prompt instructions provide persistent project context and operating rules that are automatically loaded into every session.

stem: lookup_order intermittently returns a transient "service unavailable" error. How should the tool and agent be designed for reliability?
options: Have the tool return a structured, readable error, and instruct the agent to retry a bounded number of times before escalating.
Have the tool throw an unhandled exception so the SDK aborts the run.
Have the tool return an empty success result so the conversation keeps flowing.
Have the agent fabricate plausible order details so the customer is not kept waiting.
rationale: Returning errors as structured tool results lets the model reason and retry with bounded attempts, then escalate — the reliable pattern. Option 1 crashes the session on a recoverable error. Option 2 hides failure and produces wrong answers downstream. Option 3 hallucinates data, which is unacceptable in support.
referenceText: Return errors within the tool result so the model can see them and decide how to proceed, rather than throwing; design bounded retries for transient failures.

stem: Which lookup_order tool description best follows Anthropic's tool-use guidance?
options: "Returns orders. Use it."
"Retrieves a customer's orders by customer_id, returning order_id, status, items, amount, and order date. Use after get_customer to find the specific order a refund or question refers to."
"Do not use this tool unless absolutely necessary."
"Gets stuff from the orders table via SELECT * with a join on customers."
rationale: A good description states what the tool does, its inputs and outputs, and when to use it — exactly what the model needs to choose and call it correctly. Option 0 is too sparse. Option 2 discourages proper use. Option 3 leaks implementation detail without telling the model when or how to use the tool.
referenceText: Tool descriptions should clearly explain what the tool does, what parameters it takes, what it returns, and when to use it.

stem: Across a multi-turn resolution, the agent must remember which order the customer confirmed and whether identity was verified. What is the most reliable approach?
options: Rely on the model re-deriving these facts from the raw chat transcript on every turn.
Maintain explicit structured state (e.g., verified_customer_id, confirmed_order_id) carried in context and updated as each step completes.
Store these facts only in the customer's browser and never in the agent's context.
Ask the customer to re-state their order number on every turn.
rationale: Explicit structured state makes critical facts durable and unambiguous across turns, improving reliability and safe actions. Option 0 is error-prone as history grows. Option 2 keeps the facts out of the model's reach. Option 3 shifts burden to the customer and still risks transcription errors.
referenceText: Track key decision state explicitly rather than relying on the model to re-infer it from long histories; carry structured state across turns for reliability.

stem: The four functions are exposed to Claude through a custom MCP server. In MCP terms, what makes process_refund callable by the model?
options: It is registered as a tool the server advertises via tools/list and executes on tools/call.
It is exposed only as a static resource the model reads.
It is defined as a prompt template the user manually selects.
It is compiled directly into the model's weights.
rationale: In MCP, executable, model-invoked actions are tools: the server advertises them via tools/list and runs them on tools/call. Resources (option 1) are read-only context, not actions. Prompts (option 2) are user-selected templates. Option 3 misdescribes how MCP integrations work.
referenceText: Tools in MCP are model-controlled functions the server exposes; clients discover them via tools/list and invoke them via tools/call.

stem: The agent sometimes escalates trivial questions and sometimes tries to handle cases it should escalate. Which technique most directly sharpens this boundary?
options: Increase temperature so the responses are more varied.
Provide a few worked examples (few-shot) showing inputs that should be handled versus escalated, each paired with the correct action.
Remove the escalate_to_human tool so the agent must always resolve on its own.
Tell the model in the prompt to simply be more confident.
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) examples that demonstrate the handle-vs-escalate boundary directly teach the desired decision behavior. Option 0 increases inconsistency. Option 2 removes a needed safety valve and harms correct escalation. Option 3 is vague and does not define the boundary.
referenceText: Including several examples of the desired behavior (multishot prompting) is one of the most effective ways to steer Claude toward the correct decisions.

stem: A billing dispute conversation has grown very long and is approaching the context window limit. What is the best reliability practice?
options: Let the conversation silently truncate the oldest messages and hope the key facts survive.
Compact earlier turns into a structured summary that preserves verified identity, the order in question, and actions already taken.
Immediately escalate any conversation once it passes ten turns.
Lower max_tokens so each response is shorter.
rationale: Summarizing/compacting while explicitly preserving load-bearing facts keeps the agent reliable near the context limit. Option 0 risks silently dropping critical verified facts. Option 2 escalates unnecessarily and hurts first-contact resolution. Option 4 shortens outputs but does nothing about the growing history.
referenceText: As conversations approach the context window, summarize or compact older turns while preserving the essential state needed to continue correctly.

stem: For a workflow step where the agent must fetch the customer record before doing anything else, how do you make Claude reliably call get_customer at that step?
options: Set tool_choice to require/force the get_customer tool for that request.
Permanently delete every other tool from the application.
Put "ALWAYS CALL get_customer" in all capital letters and nothing else.
Lower the temperature to 0 so a tool is always called.
rationale: The tool_choice parameter can force a specific tool, guaranteeing get_customer is invoked at that step. Option 1 destroys the agent's other capabilities. Option 2 is unreliable prompt shouting. Option 3 controls sampling randomness, not whether or which tool is called.
referenceText: Use the tool_choice parameter to force the model to call a specific tool when a step requires it.

stem: Refunds above a threshold must pass through a human approval gate before execution, without redesigning the tool itself. Which Agent SDK mechanism fits best?
options: A permission / canUseTool callback that inspects the process_refund input and can allow, deny, or route it for approval.
Removing process_refund from the tool list entirely so no refunds are possible.
A log review performed after refunds have already been issued.
Instructing the model in the prompt and fully trusting it with no enforcement.
rationale: A permission callback lets you programmatically gate tool calls based on their arguments — allow, deny, or escalate — which is exactly a policy-based approval gate. Option 1 removes the capability wholesale. Option 2 is after-the-fact and cannot prevent a bad refund. Option 3 relies on unenforced instructions for a money-moving action.
referenceText: The SDK's permission / canUseTool callback intercepts tool calls before execution so you can allow, deny, or require approval based on the tool and its input.

stem: Which two situations most clearly warrant calling escalate_to_human rather than continuing autonomously? (Select 2.)
options: The customer's identity cannot be verified but they are demanding account changes.
The customer asks a simple "where is my order" question answerable from lookup_order.
A repeated tool failure leaves the agent unable to complete a refund the customer is clearly owed.
The customer used harsh language, but the underlying request is routine and in-policy.
The customer asks the agent to confirm the store's return window, which is documented in the agent's instructions.
rationale: Unverifiable identity on a sensitive account change and an unresolvable-after-retries system failure both exceed safe autonomous handling and need a human. Options 1 and 4 are directly answerable and should be resolved to hit the first-contact target. Option 3 is emotional tone only; the request itself is routine and should be handled.
referenceText: Escalate when a request cannot be safely completed autonomously — unverifiable identity for sensitive actions, or persistent failures that block a legitimate resolution.

stem: Before issuing refunds, the agent occasionally refunds the wrong order. Which technique improves the decision without exposing internal reasoning to the customer?
options: Prompt the model to reason step by step in a scratchpad/thinking block to match the request to the correct order before calling process_refund.
Ask the customer to approve each of the model's internal thoughts before it proceeds.
Disable all tools so the model can only talk to the customer.
Always refund the customer's most recent order by default.
rationale: Chain-of-thought reasoning in a scratchpad improves accuracy on the order-matching decision while staying internal. Option 1 is nonsensical and leaks reasoning. Option 2 removes the ability to resolve anything. Option 3 is a blind heuristic that will frequently refund the wrong order.
referenceText: Encouraging step-by-step reasoning (chain of thought), optionally in a scratchpad, improves accuracy on complex or ambiguous decisions.

stem: The team wants to expose read-only reference data (store return policy, per-tier refund limits) to the agent. In MCP, which primitive is most appropriate for this static, model-readable reference data?
options: An MCP resource, which supplies context data the client can load, distinct from action-performing tools.
A process_refund-style tool, because every capability must be modeled as a tool.
A server-initiated sampling request.
It cannot be represented in MCP at all.
rationale: Resources are MCP's primitive for exposing data/context to be read, which fits static reference material. Option 1 misuses tools (which perform actions) for read-only data. Option 2 (sampling) is the server asking the model to generate, unrelated here. Option 3 is simply false.
referenceText: Resources expose data and content that clients can read and provide as context, as opposed to tools which perform actions.

stem: A network hiccup causes the agent to call process_refund twice for the same order. Which design most reliably prevents a double refund?
options: Make process_refund idempotent using an idempotency key / order reference so a duplicate call does not issue a second refund.
Instruct the model in the prompt to "never call the same tool twice."
Increase the request timeout on the refund call.
Ask the customer whether they were already refunded before each attempt.
rationale: Idempotency on the refund operation guarantees at-most-once effect regardless of retries or duplicate calls — the robust systems answer. Option 1 relies on unenforced model behavior. Option 2 does not address duplicate execution. Option 3 is unreliable and shifts a correctness guarantee onto the customer.
referenceText: Design state-changing tools to be idempotent so retries or duplicate invocations do not cause repeated side effects.

stem: get_customer shows the account has three recent orders, and the customer says only "refund my order." What is the best-designed behavior?
options: Ask one targeted clarifying question (e.g., list the three orders and ask which one) before calling process_refund.
Refund all three orders to be safe.
Refund the cheapest of the three orders to limit financial risk.
Escalate to a human simply because there is more than one order.
rationale: One targeted clarifying question resolves the ambiguity and enables correct first-contact resolution. Option 1 causes over-refunding. Option 2 is an arbitrary guess that will often be wrong. Option 3 escalates a case the agent can easily resolve, hurting the resolution target.
referenceText: When inputs are ambiguous, prefer asking a concise clarifying question over guessing before taking an irreversible action.

stem: The team wants automated regression testing that the agent still escalates and refunds correctly as prompts and tools change. Which approach fits the Claude Agent SDK / Claude Code?
options: Run the agent headlessly in CI against a suite of scripted scenarios and assert on the tools it called and the final outcomes.
Manually re-test the agent by hand right before each release only.
Rely on production customer complaints to surface regressions after launch.
Freeze the prompt permanently so that no testing is ever needed.
rationale: Headless/programmatic runs in CI with assertions on tool calls and outcomes give repeatable regression coverage as the agent evolves. Option 1 is slow and inconsistent. Option 2 detects problems only after customer impact. Option 3 is unrealistic and blocks all improvement.
referenceText: Run Claude Code / the Agent SDK headlessly (non-interactively) in CI to script and assert on agent behavior for automated evaluation.

stem: The agent sometimes states an order status that lookup_order never actually returned. Which practice most reduces this?
options: Instruct the agent to base order facts only on tool results, and to say it does not know or re-query when the data is missing rather than inferring.
Raise the temperature so the answers sound more natural.
Remove lookup_order so there is nothing for the answer to contradict.
Tell the agent to always answer with confidence.
rationale: Grounding answers strictly in tool results and allowing the model to say it doesn't know directly reduces hallucination. Option 1 makes fabrication more likely. Option 2 removes the source of truth entirely. Option 3 encourages confident hallucinations, the opposite of what is needed.
referenceText: Ground responses in retrieved data and give the model an explicit out to say it doesn't know, which reduces hallucinations.

stem: For a customer whose customer_id is already known from earlier verification, the agent needs both the customer profile (get_customer) and their recent orders (lookup_order), and neither call depends on the other's output. What improves latency while keeping correctness?
options: Allow the model to issue get_customer and lookup_order as parallel tool calls since it already has the inputs for both.
Always force strictly sequential calls with a fixed delay between them.
Merge both operations into a single process_refund call to save a round trip.
Skip lookup_order and assume the customer means their most recent order.
rationale: Independent calls with all inputs available can be issued in parallel, reducing latency without affecting correctness. Option 1 needlessly serializes independent work. Option 2 conflates unrelated read operations with a state-changing action. Option 3 drops a needed lookup and guesses.
referenceText: When a model has the inputs for multiple independent tools, it can request them as parallel tool calls to reduce latency.

stem: You are defining the custom `process_refund` MCP tool for the support agent. The agent frequently calls it with a vague `reason` string and sometimes omits the order identifier, forcing extra clarification turns. Which change to the tool definition will most reliably improve how correctly Claude invokes it?
options: Write a thorough tool `description` explaining when to use it, mark `order_id` and `amount` as required, and constrain `reason` to an enum of accepted refund reasons in the input schema
Keep the schema minimal so Claude has maximum flexibility, and rely on the system prompt alone to explain every field
Rename the tool to `do_refund_now` so the model recognizes it as high priority
Add all possible fields as optional strings so the model can decide at runtime which ones matter
rationale: Anthropic's tool-use guidance is explicit that the single most important factor in reliable tool calling is a detailed tool description plus a well-specified JSON input schema: required fields and enums remove ambiguity and let the model fill parameters correctly. A minimal schema pushes disambiguation onto free text and increases errors. Renaming does nothing for parameter correctness. Making everything an optional string removes exactly the constraints that reduce malformed calls.
referenceText: Anthropic tool-use overview: 'Provide extremely detailed descriptions' and specify input schemas

stem: The team is deciding which MCP primitive to use for `get_customer`, which the agent should call on its own whenever it needs the caller's profile during a conversation. Which MCP primitive is the correct fit, and why?
options: A tool, because tools are model-controlled actions the agent can invoke autonomously when it decides it needs the data
A resource, because resources are the only way to return structured customer data to the client
A prompt, because prompts are how servers expose reusable data-fetching logic to the model
A sampling request, because the server must ask the host LLM to generate the customer record
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled: the LLM decides when to call them to take an action or fetch data, which matches an agent autonomously retrieving a customer profile mid-conversation. Resources are application/host-controlled context that the client attaches, not something the model invokes at will. Prompts are user-initiated templates surfaced in the UI. Sampling lets a server request a completion from the host model and is unrelated to fetching a customer record.
referenceText: MCP concepts: Tools are model-controlled functions the LLM can invoke

stem: Mid-conversation, a customer disputes a charge, but `lookup_order` returns no matching order and `get_customer` shows the account was created after the disputed date. The agent cannot verify the claim with available tools. What is the most appropriate reliability behavior?
options: Call `escalate_to_human` with a structured summary of what was attempted and why it could not be resolved
Call `process_refund` for the disputed amount to avoid a poor customer experience
Apologize and end the conversation, since the tools returned no matching order
Retry `lookup_order` repeatedly until it eventually returns the order
rationale: The scenario's goal is first-contact resolution with correct escalation. When the agent lacks the data to act safely, the reliable pattern is a clean, structured handoff to a human that preserves context, which is exactly what `escalate_to_human` is for. Issuing a refund without verification is an unsafe action on unverifiable data. Ending the chat abandons the customer. Blindly retrying a tool that returns no match will not create data that does not exist and wastes turns.
referenceText: Agent SDK: designing escalation and safe handoff paths for agents

stem: For auditing, every refund decision must be captured as a machine-readable object with fields `decision`, `order_id`, `amount`, and `reason_code`. You want to guarantee the model emits this structure rather than prose. What is the most reliable technique with the Claude API?
options: Define a tool whose input schema is exactly the required object and use `tool_choice` to require that tool, so the decision comes back as validated tool input
Ask in the system prompt for JSON and parse whatever text the model returns, discarding responses that fail to parse
Instruct the model to 'always respond in JSON' and lower the temperature to 0 so output is deterministic
Append a few-shot example of the JSON at the end of every user message and hope the model mirrors it
rationale: Tool use is Anthropic's recommended mechanism for guaranteed structured output: define a tool whose input_schema is the desired object and force it with tool_choice, and the model's arguments arrive as schema-shaped JSON you can validate. Prompting for JSON and parsing text is fragile and can still return prose or preamble. Temperature 0 improves determinism but does not guarantee schema conformance. Few-shot examples help but do not enforce the structure the way a required tool does.
referenceText: Anthropic: using tool use / JSON schema to enforce structured output

stem: The custom MCP tools occasionally fail: `lookup_order` times out, or `process_refund` returns a downstream payment error. Which practices make the agent robust to these failures? (Select 3.)
options: Return errors to the model inside a `tool_result` with `is_error` set to true and a short diagnostic message, so the model can react
Distinguish transient errors (retry with backoff) from terminal errors (stop and escalate) rather than retrying everything
Escalate to a human when a critical action like `process_refund` fails after safe retries are exhausted
Suppress all tool errors and return an empty result so the conversation never surfaces a failure
Have the agent invent a plausible confirmation number so the customer receives an immediate answer
rationale: Reliable agents surface failures back to the model as tool_result errors (is_error=true) so Claude can adapt; classify errors so only transient ones are retried while terminal ones stop-and-escalate; and escalate to a human when a critical financial action cannot complete. Suppressing errors with empty results hides real failures and produces wrong resolutions. Inventing a confirmation number is a fabrication that harms the customer and violates grounding principles.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use: handling errors and returning tool_result with is_error

stem: The engineering team maintains the agent's repository with Claude Code. They want durable, project-specific guidance — the refund policy limits, the exact order in which to call `get_customer` then `lookup_order`, and when escalation is mandatory — automatically available to Claude in every session without pasting it each time. Where should this live?
options: In a `CLAUDE.md` file at the project root, which Claude Code loads into context automatically at session start
In a comment block at the top of the largest source file, so it is read whenever that file is opened
In each developer's personal shell profile as an environment variable
In the git commit messages, so the policy is captured in history
rationale: CLAUDE.md is the memory file Claude Code reads automatically at the start of a session, making it the right home for durable project conventions, workflows, and policy the agent should always follow. A source-file comment is only seen if that file happens to be read. A shell env var is not project memory and is not surfaced as guidance. Commit messages are historical and not loaded as working context.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: CLAUDE.md loaded automatically as project memory

stem: Customers describe returns ambiguously ('the thing I bought last week is broken'). The agent must decide among refund, replacement, or escalation. Which prompt-engineering technique most directly improves consistent, correct classification of these ambiguous requests?
options: Provide several few-shot examples in the system prompt that map representative ambiguous phrasings to the correct action and tool call
Increase the max_tokens limit so the model has room to consider more possibilities
Remove the tool descriptions so the model relies purely on its own judgment
Ask the customer to re-state their request in a fixed template before the agent does anything
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) prompting is Anthropic's recommended way to make classification of messy, ambiguous inputs consistent: concrete examples pin down how to map phrasings to the desired action. Raising max_tokens affects output length, not decision quality. Removing tool descriptions degrades tool-calling reliability. Forcing customers into a template harms experience and does not teach the model the mapping.
referenceText: Anthropic prompt engineering: use examples (multishot prompting)

stem: A customer sends the same 'where is my refund' message twice in quick succession while the agent is still processing the first request. Without safeguards, the agent could call `process_refund` twice for one order. Which design most reliably prevents a duplicate refund?
options: Have the agent track processed order IDs in structured conversation state and make `process_refund` idempotent using the order ID as an idempotency key
Instruct the model in the system prompt to 'never refund twice' and rely on it to remember
Lower the temperature to 0 so the model behaves deterministically
Add a second confirmation question before every refund so the customer catches the duplicate
rationale: Duplicate-action prevention is a reliability/state problem, solved by tracking what has already been done in structured state and making the write operation idempotent (keyed on order ID) so a repeated call is a no-op. A system-prompt instruction is not a guarantee and can be forgotten across turns. Temperature 0 affects sampling, not action idempotency. An extra confirmation adds friction and still relies on the model, not a hard guard.
referenceText: Agent SDK / reliability: structured state and idempotent side-effecting tools

stem: When constructing the agent on the Claude Agent SDK, where should you place the standing instructions that define the agent's role ('a careful support resolution agent'), its tone, and the policy that refunds over $500 always require escalation?
options: In the system prompt, which sets the agent's persistent role, constraints, and behavior for the whole session
In the first user message only, so the model treats it as the customer's request
Embedded inside the `process_refund` tool's return value
In the assistant's first turn as narration the model writes to itself
rationale: The system prompt is the correct place for durable role, tone, and policy constraints; it governs the whole session and is the recommended way to give Claude a role and rules. Putting it in a user message conflates policy with the customer's request and can be overridden. A tool's return value only appears after a call and cannot govern behavior beforehand. Assistant self-narration is not a reliable place to store constraints.
referenceText: Anthropic: give Claude a role with a system prompt

stem: You are writing the JSON input schemas for `get_customer` and `lookup_order`. Which schema-design choices improve the reliability of Claude's tool calls? (Select 2.)
options: Give each parameter a clear `description` and use `required` to mark the fields the tool genuinely needs (e.g., `customer_id`, `order_id`)
Use enums or typed formats for constrained fields (e.g., an `order_status` enum) instead of free-form strings
Make every parameter a free-form string and mark none as required, to give the model total freedom
Accept a single opaque `query` string per tool and parse it yourself later so the schema stays tiny
Duplicate the same parameters across both tools even when unused, to keep the schemas symmetric
rationale: Clear per-parameter descriptions with accurate required fields, plus enums/typed formats for constrained values, are the documented practices that make Claude fill tool arguments correctly. Making everything an optional free-form string removes the constraints that guide the model. A single opaque query string throws away schema-level validation and re-introduces parsing ambiguity. Duplicating unused parameters just adds noise and invites the model to populate irrelevant fields.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use: input_schema design and detailed parameter descriptions

stem: Your MCP server wraps a flaky billing API. When `process_refund` fails downstream, how should the tool signal the failure back to Claude so the agent handles it well?
options: Return a `tool_result` for that `tool_use` with `is_error: true` and a concise message describing what failed
Throw an exception in the server so the whole SDK request aborts and the customer sees a stack trace
Return a normal success `tool_result` with empty content so the model assumes it worked
Return the raw HTTP 500 body of the payment API as the tool result with no error flag
rationale: The tool-use protocol expects failures to be reported as a tool_result carrying is_error=true and a brief description, which lets Claude recognize the failure and choose to retry or escalate. Aborting the whole request removes the model's chance to recover gracefully. Returning a fake success causes the model to tell the customer the refund succeeded when it did not. Dumping a raw 500 body without the error flag can be misread as valid content.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use: reporting tool errors with is_error in tool_result

stem: The four custom tools (`get_customer`, `lookup_order`, `process_refund`, `escalate_to_human`) run as an in-house service on the same host as the agent runtime, with no need for remote network exposure. Which MCP transport is the natural fit for connecting the agent to this local server?
options: The stdio transport, where the host launches the server as a subprocess and communicates over standard input/output
A public WebSocket endpoint exposed to the internet so any client can reach the tools
Email, with the server polling an inbox for tool requests
A shared spreadsheet the agent reads and writes to invoke tools
rationale: MCP defines stdio as the standard transport for local servers run as a subprocess by the host, which matches a co-located in-house tool service. A public WebSocket needlessly exposes internal refund tooling to the internet and is not required for a local integration. Email polling and shared spreadsheets are not MCP transports and provide none of the protocol's request/response semantics.
referenceText: MCP transports: stdio for local subprocess servers

stem: For complex billing disputes, the agent sometimes jumps to `process_refund` before checking the order. You want it to reason through eligibility first. Which approach best encourages correct sequencing without hard-coding every branch?
options: Prompt the model to think step by step about eligibility (gather customer, verify order, check policy) before deciding which tool to call
Set `tool_choice` to always force `process_refund` on the first turn
Remove `lookup_order` so the model cannot get distracted by order checks
Increase temperature so the model explores more diverse actions
rationale: Chain-of-thought prompting — asking the model to reason through the steps before acting — is the recommended way to improve multi-step decisions and reduce premature actions. Forcing process_refund removes the model's judgment entirely and guarantees unverified refunds. Removing lookup_order deletes the exact verification step you need. Higher temperature increases variability, not correct sequencing.
referenceText: Anthropic prompt engineering: let Claude think (chain of thought)

stem: A customer asks for the delivery date of an order. `lookup_order` returns a record with no `delivery_date` field populated. The agent must respond. What is the correct grounded behavior?
options: State that the delivery date is not available and offer to look into it or escalate, rather than inventing a plausible date
Provide a best-guess date based on typical shipping times so the customer gets a concrete answer
Silently call `process_refund` since a missing delivery date implies a lost package
Repeat the customer's question back and wait, taking no tool action
rationale: Grounding guidance says the agent should rely on tool-returned facts and explicitly acknowledge when information is unavailable instead of fabricating it, which prevents hallucinated commitments to customers. Guessing a date is exactly the hallucination the guidance warns against. Refunding on an inference is an unsafe, unrelated action. Merely echoing the question fails to help and gives no path forward.
referenceText: Anthropic: reduce hallucinations by grounding answers and allowing 'I don't know'

stem: While iterating on the agent in Claude Code, developers repeatedly run the same multi-step task: replay a sample transcript through the tools and check escalation logic. They want a reusable, parameterized command they can invoke by name in the Claude Code session. What should they create?
options: A custom slash command defined as a Markdown prompt file in `.claude/commands/`, invocable as `/replay-transcript`
A new MCP server dedicated solely to storing that one prompt
A CLAUDE.md that they manually copy-paste into chat each time they want to run it
A git branch named after the task
rationale: Custom slash commands in Claude Code are exactly this: reusable prompt files (in .claude/commands/) invoked by name, optionally with arguments, ideal for repeated workflows. Spinning up an MCP server to hold a single prompt is heavy misuse of the primitive. CLAUDE.md is passive project memory, not an on-demand command. A git branch organizes code, not reusable prompts.
referenceText: Claude Code: custom slash commands in .claude/commands

stem: You are codifying when the agent must call `escalate_to_human` to protect first-contact-resolution quality. Which conditions are appropriate mandatory-escalation triggers? (Select 3.)
options: The requested action exceeds the agent's policy authority (e.g., a refund above the configured limit)
Required data cannot be verified with the available tools after reasonable attempts
A critical tool like `process_refund` fails terminally and cannot be completed
The customer's message contains a typo the agent is unsure how to interpret literally
The customer greets the agent politely at the start of the conversation
rationale: Sound escalation triggers are: actions beyond the agent's authority, unverifiable data after reasonable attempts, and terminal failure of a critical action — each is a case where autonomous resolution is unsafe or impossible. A minor typo is normally resolvable by the model itself and should not trigger a handoff. A polite greeting is routine conversation, not an escalation condition; escalating on it would tank first-contact resolution.
referenceText: Agent SDK: designing escalation criteria for safe autonomous operation

stem: In a workflow step, once the agent has gathered enough context and decided a case is out of policy, you want to guarantee it emits an escalation rather than continuing to chat. Which API mechanism enforces that a specific tool is used on that turn?
options: Set `tool_choice` to require the `escalate_to_human` tool for that request
Delete every other tool from the request permanently for the rest of the session
Put 'you must escalate' in the customer's message
Raise `max_tokens` so the model has space to escalate
rationale: tool_choice lets you force the model to call a specific tool on a given request, which is the precise control for guaranteeing an escalation once the decision is made. Permanently deleting the other tools breaks the rest of the conversation and is far more destructive than scoping one turn. Injecting text into the customer message is not a reliable enforcement mechanism. max_tokens governs length, not tool selection.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use: forcing tool use with tool_choice

stem: Before letting Claude Code make sweeping edits to the agent's escalation-routing module, a developer wants Claude to first outline the changes it intends to make and get approval, without editing files yet. Which Claude Code capability fits this need?
options: Plan mode, which has Claude research and propose an approach for approval before making any changes
Headless mode, so the change runs unattended in CI with no review
Deleting the CLAUDE.md so Claude has fewer constraints while editing
Running the agent in production and observing what breaks
rationale: Plan mode is designed for exactly this: Claude investigates and presents a plan for the human to approve before it starts modifying files, which is appropriate for a sensitive module. Headless mode runs non-interactively and is the opposite of review-before-edit. Removing CLAUDE.md strips guardrails and does not add review. Testing directly in production is reckless for escalation-routing logic.
referenceText: Claude Code: plan mode for proposing changes before editing

stem: Some support conversations run very long, and the transcript plus repeated tool results are approaching the context window limit, degrading the agent's later responses. Which reliability technique best preserves the agent's effectiveness across a long session?
options: Maintain compact structured state (customer, verified order, decisions so far) and summarize/compact older turns so essential context is retained within the window
Continue appending every raw tool result verbatim and let the oldest messages silently fall off
Restart the conversation from scratch whenever it gets long, discarding all prior context
Increase temperature so the model compensates for lost context
rationale: Long-running agents stay reliable by managing context: keeping a compact structured record of the salient facts and summarizing/compacting older turns so the important state survives within the window. Letting old messages silently truncate can drop critical details like a verified order ID. Restarting from scratch throws away resolution progress and hurts first-contact resolution. Temperature does not recover lost context.
referenceText: Agent SDK: context management, compaction, and structured state

stem: At the start of many conversations the agent independently needs both the customer profile and the recent order to reason about a return. `get_customer` and `lookup_order` are independent reads with no data dependency between them. What is the most efficient correct pattern?
options: Allow the model to request both tools in parallel in a single turn, since neither depends on the other's output
Force strict sequential calls, always waiting for `get_customer` to fully complete before even considering `lookup_order`
Merge the two into one tool that returns everything, even when only one is needed
Skip `get_customer` entirely and infer the profile from the order record
rationale: Claude can request multiple independent tools in the same turn, and parallelizing independent reads reduces latency without sacrificing correctness — appropriate when neither call depends on the other. Forcing sequential execution adds latency for no benefit here. Merging into one always-fat tool fetches unneeded data and reduces flexibility. Inferring the profile from the order risks missing or incorrect customer data and undermines grounding.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use: parallel tool calls for independent operations

stem: Your support agent must always verify a customer with get_customer before it is allowed to call process_refund. Across ambiguous conversations, what is the most reliable way to enforce this ordering while keeping the agent flexible?
options: Set tool_choice to {type:"tool", name:"get_customer"} on every turn so the model can never select another tool first
Document the dependency in each tool's description and the system prompt (e.g., "process_refund requires a verified customer_id returned by get_customer") and keep tool_choice on auto
Mutate the tool list on the MCP server between turns, removing process_refund until get_customer has returned
Silently drop any process_refund tool call the client receives before a get_customer call has completed
rationale: Correct: ordering constraints are best expressed as clear tool descriptions plus system-prompt guidance, letting the model reason about the dependency while tool_choice stays on auto so normal flow works. Distractor 0 pins the model to one tool forever and breaks the conversation. Distractor 2 (schema mutation between turns) is over-engineered and brittle. Distractor 3 silently dropping calls produces confusing loops with no feedback to the model.
referenceText: Tool descriptions and the system prompt are the primary levers for shaping when and how Claude calls tools; write detailed, action-oriented descriptions.

stem: process_refund calls sometimes time out at the network layer even though the refund succeeded on the backend. A naive retry risks refunding the customer twice. What is the best design to make retries safe?
options: Configure a very long HTTP timeout so the call effectively never times out and retries become unnecessary
Have process_refund accept an idempotency_key (derived from the order and attempt) so a retried call after a timeout returns the original result instead of issuing a second refund
Instruct the agent in the system prompt to never retry process_refund under any circumstances
Wrap process_refund in an automatic three-times retry loop on the MCP server with no deduplication
rationale: Correct: an idempotency key lets the backend recognize a duplicate request and return the original outcome, making retries safe. Distractor 0 only reduces timeout frequency and still leaves duplicate-charge risk on any failure. Distractor 2 forbids all retries, harming reliability on genuinely transient failures. Distractor 3 blindly retries and actively causes double refunds.
referenceText: Design tools that change state to be idempotent and to return clear structured results so the agent and callers can recover from partial failures.

stem: You are defining the input_schema for process_refund so Claude produces well-formed calls in high-ambiguity conversations. Which schema design best prevents malformed or unsafe calls?
options: Accept a single free-text string and parse the amount and reason out of it on the server
Define an input_schema with required order_id (string), amount (number), and reason as a string enum of allowed values, so malformed calls are rejected before execution
Make every field optional so the agent can call the tool even when it is missing information
Use additionalProperties:true with no required fields to maximize the agent's flexibility
rationale: Correct: a typed JSON schema with required fields and an enum constrains the model's output and rejects invalid calls up front, which is the point of structured tool inputs. Distractor 0 pushes fragile parsing onto the server and loses schema validation. Distractor 2 lets the agent fire refunds with missing critical data. Distractor 3 removes all guardrails and invites malformed input.
referenceText: Tools use a JSON Schema input_schema; specifying required fields and enums constrains and validates what the model can pass.

stem: The agent frequently needs to decide whether to call get_customer. What does Claude actually rely on to make that decision?
options: The tool's internal implementation language and the server port it runs on
A clear, action-oriented tool name plus a description stating what get_customer does, when to use it, and what it returns
The order in which tools were registered on the MCP server
Hidden server-side metadata that is never sent to the model
rationale: Correct: the model only sees each tool's name, description, and input schema, so a precise description of purpose, timing, and return value drives correct selection. Distractor 0 is irrelevant implementation detail the model never sees. Distractor 2 registration order is not a documented selection signal. Distractor 3 hidden metadata is by definition not available to the model.
referenceText: Claude decides which tool to use based on the name, description, and input schema it is given; detailed descriptions are the single most important factor for good performance.

stem: When the agent calls escalate_to_human, you want a clean handoff so the human is not starting from scratch. Which two items should the structured escalation state include? (Select 2.)
options: A full snapshot of the model's weights
A concise summary of the customer's issue and the steps and tools already attempted
The verified customer_id and the relevant order_id(s) gathered during the session
The agent's raw internal reasoning tokens passed through verbatim with no filtering
A randomly generated ticket color used only for UI theming
rationale: Correct: a useful handoff carries a summary of the problem and prior attempts plus the verified identifiers the human needs to act. Distractor 0 is nonsensical. Distractor 3 dumping raw chain-of-thought is noisy and unreliable as a record. Distractor 4 is cosmetic and adds no value to resolution.
referenceText: Preserve structured state across steps and handoffs so context (identity, prior actions, current goal) is not lost; summarize rather than passing raw reasoning.

stem: Returns cases are often ambiguous (multiple orders, unclear item). You want the agent to ask one targeted clarifying question before acting. How should you use few-shot examples to encode this behavior?
options: Include examples that always immediately call process_refund to maximize speed
Include a few examples that demonstrate asking one targeted clarifying question when the order is ambiguous, before selecting a tool
Provide fifty near-identical examples of the same refund case to strongly reinforce the pattern
Avoid examples entirely because they bias the model toward memorized outputs
rationale: Correct: a small set of well-chosen examples that model the desired disambiguation behavior is exactly what multishot prompting is for. Distractor 0 teaches the wrong reflex (refund without clarifying). Distractor 2 overfits to one case and wastes context without covering variety. Distractor 3 discards a proven technique; relevant, diverse examples improve reliability.
referenceText: Multishot (few-shot) prompting with a few relevant, diverse examples steers the model toward the desired behavior and format.

stem: Your team is deploying this support agent on the Claude Agent SDK and debating where the escalation policy and tone rules should live. What is the correct placement?
options: In a CLAUDE.md file, because the Agent SDK automatically loads it at runtime for every deployment
In the system prompt supplied to the Agent SDK, since CLAUDE.md is a Claude Code memory convention rather than runtime configuration for a deployed support agent
Only inside each tool's description, leaving the system prompt empty
Baked into the model via fine-tuning, because system prompts cannot express multi-step policy
rationale: Correct: policy, escalation rules, and tone for a deployed SDK agent belong in its system prompt; CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's memory-file convention, not a runtime config the SDK auto-loads for arbitrary deployments. Distractor 0 misattributes CLAUDE.md behavior. Distractor 2 scatters policy into tool descriptions and loses coherent guidance. Distractor 3 is unnecessary and false; system prompts readily express multi-step policy.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's memory mechanism; deployed agents are configured through the system prompt and tools passed to the Agent SDK.

stem: Some billing conversations run long enough to approach the context window. What is the best way to preserve key facts (verified identity, prior refund attempts) without exceeding the limit?
options: Send the entire raw transcript every turn and rely on the model to ignore old, irrelevant turns
Compact older turns into a running structured summary/state while keeping recent turns verbatim, so key facts survive within the window
Truncate the conversation by dropping the system prompt first when space runs low
Start a brand-new conversation on every user message to keep the context small
rationale: Correct: summarizing/compacting older turns into structured state while keeping recent turns verbatim retains critical facts and stays within the window. Distractor 0 will eventually overflow and increases cost and distraction. Distractor 2 dropping the system prompt removes the agent's policy and behavior. Distractor 3 discards all context and defeats first-contact resolution.
referenceText: Manage long conversations by summarizing or compacting earlier context into structured state rather than resending or truncating critical instructions.

stem: lookup_order sometimes cannot find an order the customer describes. How should the tool return this so the agent can recover gracefully?
options: Return a normal successful result with empty fields so the conversation keeps moving
Return a tool_result with is_error set and a clear message such as ORDER_NOT_FOUND, so the agent can ask the customer for more detail or escalate
Throw an unhandled exception that terminates the SDK session
Return a 500-line stack trace as the tool result content
rationale: Correct: returning an error tool_result with is_error and a concise, meaningful code lets the model detect the failure and recover (re-ask or escalate). Distractor 0 hides the failure and leads the agent to act on missing data. Distractor 2 crashes the whole session. Distractor 3 floods the context with noise and no actionable signal.
referenceText: Return tool errors by setting is_error to true with a clear message so Claude can understand and recover from the failure.

stem: You are wiring the four custom tools (get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, escalate_to_human) into the Claude Agent SDK. What is the appropriate integration approach?
options: Paste each tool's behavior as text into the system prompt, since MCP cannot expose custom tools
Expose the four tools from an MCP server that the Agent SDK connects to, so they are advertised to the model through the protocol
Fine-tune a separate model for each tool so it becomes callable
Accept that MCP is read-only, so process_refund cannot be an MCP tool
rationale: Correct: MCP is the standard way to expose custom tools to an SDK agent; the server advertises tools that the model can then invoke. Distractor 0 misunderstands MCP's purpose. Distractor 2 fine-tuning per tool is unnecessary and wrong. Distractor 3 is false; MCP tools routinely perform state-changing actions like refunds.
referenceText: MCP servers expose tools (including state-changing actions) that clients such as the Agent SDK connect to and make available to the model.

stem: To hit 80%+ first-contact resolution without wrongly refunding customers, you must prevent the agent from asserting order facts it did not verify. What is the correct safeguard?
options: Trust the model to recall order and account details from its training data to save an extra tool call
Require the agent to obtain order and customer details from live get_customer/lookup_order results before confirming any refund, never stating order facts from memory
Set temperature to 0, which guarantees the model cannot hallucinate order data
Add a disclaimer telling customers the model may invent order details
rationale: Correct: grounding every factual claim in live tool results is the reliable defense against hallucinated order data before a refund. Distractor 0 invites fabricated details, since the model has no knowledge of this customer's orders. Distractor 2 lowering temperature reduces variance but does not guarantee factual grounding. Distractor 3 shifts blame to the customer instead of preventing the error.
referenceText: Ground the model's responses in retrieved tool results rather than its parametric memory to avoid hallucinated facts.

stem: The agent escalates too often, hurting first-contact resolution, but you must keep escalation correct for the cases that truly need a human. Which two changes best address this? (Select 2.)
options: Remove escalate_to_human entirely so nothing can be escalated
Give explicit, testable escalation criteria in the system prompt (e.g., refund over the policy threshold, repeated verification failures, legal or complaint language)
Provide few-shot examples contrasting cases the agent should resolve itself with cases it must escalate
Instruct the agent to escalate whenever it is even slightly unsure, to maximize safety
Set tool_choice to force escalate_to_human on every turn
rationale: Correct: precise, testable escalation criteria plus contrasting few-shot examples teach the agent to resolve routine cases while still escalating the right ones. Distractor 0 removes a required safety valve. Distractor 3 maximizes over-escalation, the exact problem. Distractor 4 forces escalation on every turn, breaking the agent entirely.
referenceText: Use clear, specific instructions and contrasting examples to define decision boundaries such as when to escalate versus resolve.

stem: In a regression test you want to guarantee that a specific test call exercises get_customer. Which statement about tool_choice is correct?
options: tool_choice defaults to forcing the first tool in the list, so ordering the list is enough
For a controlled test you can set tool_choice to {type:"tool", name:"get_customer"} to require that tool on that call, while keeping auto in production so the model chooses appropriately
tool_choice {type:"any"} forces get_customer specifically
Setting tool_choice to none still lets the model call get_customer
rationale: Correct: {type:"tool", name:...} forces a named tool for that call, which is useful for deterministic tests, while auto is right for production. Distractor 0 is false; the default is auto, not first-in-list. Distractor 2 "any" forces some tool but not a specific one. Distractor 3 "none" prohibits tool calls, so get_customer cannot be called.
referenceText: tool_choice supports auto, any, none, and a specific {type:"tool", name} to force a named tool for a request.

stem: For a billing case the agent needs both the customer record and the order record, and these lookups are independent. How can the agent minimize latency?
options: Tools must always run one per turn, so parallel calls are impossible
Claude can request get_customer and lookup_order in parallel within a single turn when they are independent, and you return both tool_results together, reducing round trips
Running independent tools in parallel corrupts the context and must be disabled
Parallel tool use requires a separately fine-tuned model
rationale: Correct: Claude can emit multiple independent tool_use blocks in one turn; returning both results together cuts latency. Distractor 0 is false; parallel tool use is supported. Distractor 2 invents a corruption risk that does not exist for independent calls. Distractor 3 is false; no special model is needed.
referenceText: Claude can call multiple independent tools in parallel within a single turn; return all corresponding tool_result blocks together.

stem: Downstream analytics needs a reliable record of each resolution (status, action taken, whether escalated). How should the agent emit this at the end of a conversation?
options: Return the outcome as prose and regex-parse it downstream
Use a tool or structured-output schema that captures resolution_status, action_taken, and escalated as typed fields, so downstream systems receive reliable structured data
Rely on the model always formatting valid JSON in free text without any schema
Store only the raw transcript and have humans read every case to determine the outcome
rationale: Correct: forcing a typed schema (via a tool or structured output) yields consistent, machine-readable results. Distractor 0 regex-parsing prose is brittle. Distractor 2 free-text JSON without a schema is not reliably valid. Distractor 3 does not scale and defeats automation.
referenceText: Use tool use to force structured, schema-conformant JSON output instead of parsing free-form text.

stem: Before shipping, you want automated confidence that the agent resolves and escalates correctly across representative cases. What is the best testing approach with the Agent SDK?
options: The agent can only be validated manually through an interactive UI
Run the Agent SDK in headless mode in CI against a suite of representative scenarios with mocked tool responses, asserting correct resolution and escalation
Deploy straight to production and treat customer complaints as the only test signal
Disable all tools during testing, which means tool behavior cannot be validated
rationale: Correct: running the SDK headlessly in CI against scripted scenarios with mocked tools gives repeatable assertions on behavior before release. Distractor 0 is false; automated headless testing is supported. Distractor 2 tests in production at customers' expense. Distractor 3 needlessly disables the very behavior under test.
referenceText: The Agent SDK / Claude Code can run headlessly, enabling automated, non-interactive testing in CI pipelines.

stem: Company policy caps automated refunds at a fixed amount; anything higher must go to a human. Where should this limit be enforced most reliably?
options: Only in the system prompt wording, assuming the model always complies
In the process_refund implementation as a server-side check that rejects over-threshold amounts and returns an error instructing escalation, in addition to prompt guidance
By trusting the customer to state whether the refund is within policy
By setting temperature to 0 to guarantee policy compliance
rationale: Correct: a hard financial limit belongs in the tool's server-side logic (defense in depth), which cannot be argued around, with prompt guidance layered on top. Distractor 0 relies solely on model compliance, which is not guaranteed. Distractor 2 trusts an unverified party. Distractor 3 misunderstands temperature, which does not enforce policy.
referenceText: Enforce critical constraints deterministically in tool implementations rather than relying on the model to always follow instructions.

stem: You are writing the system prompt for this high-ambiguity support agent. Which approach best improves reliable behavior?
options: Keep the system prompt vague so the model can flexibly interpret each case
Clearly state the agent's role, the available tools, the resolution goal, and hard constraints (e.g., verify identity before any refund), since explicit instructions improve reliability
List only the tool names with no behavioral guidance
Fill the prompt with unrelated company history to provide broad context
rationale: Correct: being clear and direct about role, tools, goal, and constraints is the documented way to get reliable agent behavior. Distractor 0 vagueness increases inconsistent handling of ambiguous cases. Distractor 2 omits the guidance the agent needs. Distractor 3 wastes context and distracts from the task.
referenceText: Be clear and direct: explicitly state the role, goal, available tools, and constraints to get reliable, consistent behavior.

stem: The agent should be able to consult the current returns policy document, which is read-only reference material. In MCP terms, how should this be modeled versus the actions the agent performs?
options: Expose the returns policy as a tool with side effects, like process_refund
Expose read-only reference material such as the returns policy as an MCP resource, while state-changing actions like process_refund remain tools
Treat resources and tools as identical, since MCP makes no distinction
Conclude that policy documents cannot be provided to the model at all
rationale: Correct: MCP resources are for read-only context/data, while tools are for actions with effects; the policy doc fits resources and refunds fit tools. Distractor 0 wrongly makes reference data an action tool. Distractor 2 is false; MCP explicitly distinguishes resources from tools. Distractor 3 is false; documents can be provided as resources.
referenceText: MCP resources expose read-only data/context to the model, while tools represent actions the model can invoke.

stem: Leadership wants to know whether the agent truly meets the 80%+ first-contact resolution target with correct escalation. What is the best way to measure this?
options: Measure only average response latency and ignore whether the issue was actually resolved
Build an eval set of labeled scenarios and measure first-contact resolution rate plus escalation correctness (precision and recall) against ground truth
Count the number of tool calls per conversation as the sole quality metric
Have the model grade its own transcripts as the only measure of success
rationale: Correct: a labeled eval set with resolution rate and escalation precision/recall directly measures the stated goals. Distractor 0 latency ignores correctness. Distractor 2 tool-call count is a weak proxy unrelated to resolution quality. Distractor 3 unchecked self-grading is unreliable as the sole signal.
referenceText: Define success with representative, labeled test cases and measure task-specific metrics rather than proxies.

stem: A team lead notices that every developer on the project has to re-explain the same build commands, code style, and testing conventions to Claude Code at the start of each session. She wants Claude to load these conventions automatically for everyone who checks out the repo. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md file at the repository root containing the conventions, build commands, and style guidance
Ask each developer to paste the conventions into the first prompt of every session
Store the conventions in a .env file so Claude reads them as environment variables
Create a custom slash command that developers must run before doing any work
rationale: A project-level CLAUDE.md checked into the repo root is automatically pulled into context at session start and is shared across everyone who clones the repo, making it the canonical place for build commands, style, and conventions. Pasting into every prompt (1) is exactly the repetitive burden being eliminated. A .env file (2) is for environment variables, not model context, and Claude does not treat it as memory. A slash command (3) still requires a manual, remembered action every session and is better for reusable prompt workflows than for always-on project context.
referenceText: Claude Code memory / CLAUDE.md

stem: An architect is designing the CLAUDE.md memory strategy for a large monorepo. Which statements about how Claude Code loads and composes memory files are correct? (Select 2.)
options: A CLAUDE.md in a subdirectory can supplement the project-root CLAUDE.md when Claude works with files in that subtree
A CLAUDE.md file can pull in additional files using @path imports so shared guidance is not duplicated
Only one CLAUDE.md file may exist per repository; additional files are ignored
Memory files are re-parsed from disk on every single tool call to guarantee freshness
CLAUDE.md is only read when the user explicitly runs a /memory command
rationale: Claude Code supports a hierarchy of memory including project-root and nested subdirectory CLAUDE.md files that apply when working in that subtree (0), and files can use @path imports to include other files so guidance is not duplicated (1). Option (2) is false: multiple memory files are supported across the hierarchy. Option (3) is false: memory is loaded into context at session start, not re-parsed per tool call. Option (4) is false: project/user CLAUDE.md loads automatically, not only via an explicit command.
referenceText: Claude Code memory hierarchy and imports

stem: A developer is about to have Claude Code perform a risky, multi-file refactor of authentication logic in a production repository. Before any files are changed, she wants Claude to research the codebase and present a step-by-step approach that she can review and approve. Which Claude Code capability directly supports this?
options: Plan mode, which lets Claude analyze and propose an approach without making edits until the plan is approved
Headless mode with the -p flag, which runs the refactor unattended
The /clear command, which resets the conversation before editing
A CLAUDE.md rule instructing Claude to never edit files
rationale: Plan mode is designed exactly for read-and-propose workflows: Claude investigates and produces a plan without applying edits until the user approves, which fits a high-risk refactor that needs review first. Headless -p (1) is for unattended/automated execution, the opposite of review-before-change. /clear (2) only wipes conversation context. A CLAUDE.md 'never edit' rule (3) would block the eventual refactor entirely and is a blunt, fragile instruction rather than a review gate.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows (plan mode)
