stem: A team wants a reusable '/security-review' command that runs the same detailed prompt against whatever file path the developer passes in. Where should they define this command and how should the file reference the passed-in path?
options: Create .claude/commands/security-review.md containing the prompt and reference the input with $ARGUMENTS
Add a shell alias named security-review in the developer's shell profile
Define it in package.json under a scripts.security-review key
Put the prompt text directly in CLAUDE.md under a heading called security-review
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands/ (project) or ~/.claude/commands/ (personal); the filename becomes the command name and $ARGUMENTS injects whatever the user typed after the command, so /security-review src/auth.ts passes that path in. A shell alias (1) cannot drive a Claude session prompt. package.json scripts (2) are for npm, unrelated to slash commands. Putting the prompt in CLAUDE.md (3) makes it always-on context, not an invokable, parameterized command.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands and $ARGUMENTS

stem: A DevOps engineer wants Claude Code to run in a CI pipeline: given a diff, it should generate a summary and exit non-interactively, printing only its final text output. Which invocation is appropriate?
options: Run claude -p "summarize this diff" (print/headless mode) so it executes non-interactively and returns the result
Launch the interactive TUI and script keystrokes to type the prompt
Use /compact inside an interactive session to shorten the output
Rely on plan mode so CI can approve the plan before continuing
rationale: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code in headless/non-interactive mode, executing a single prompt and emitting output suitable for scripting and CI, which is exactly the requirement. Scripting keystrokes into the interactive TUI (1) is brittle and unsupported as an automation pattern. /compact (2) summarizes conversation context, not a CI mechanism. Plan mode (3) requires interactive human approval, which defeats unattended CI.
referenceText: Claude Code CLI reference (headless / print mode)

stem: A team wants Claude to automatically respond to '@claude' mentions in pull request comments, make requested changes, and push commits back to the PR branch. What is the intended way to set this up?
options: Install the Claude Code GitHub Action and configure it with an API key secret in the repository
Give each reviewer a personal API key and ask them to run Claude locally on every PR
Add a CLAUDE.md instruction telling Claude to watch GitHub for mentions
Enable plan mode so the PR author can approve Claude's edits in the terminal
rationale: The Claude Code GitHub Action is purpose-built to react to @claude mentions in issues and PRs, run in GitHub's runners, and commit changes, configured via repository secrets. Manual local runs (1) do not provide the automated mention-triggered workflow. A CLAUDE.md instruction (2) cannot make Claude poll GitHub — memory files only shape in-session behavior. Plan mode (3) is an interactive terminal feature, unrelated to reacting to PR comments.
referenceText: Claude Code GitHub Actions

stem: A developer wants Claude Code to be able to query the team's Sentry issues and Postgres schema during debugging sessions, using existing servers that expose those capabilities over the Model Context Protocol. What is the correct approach?
options: Register the MCP servers with Claude Code (e.g., via claude mcp add) so their tools/resources become available in sessions
Paste the Sentry and Postgres documentation into CLAUDE.md so Claude can guess the data
Ask Claude to open a browser and log into Sentry manually each session
Rewrite the servers as custom slash commands under .claude/commands
rationale: MCP lets Claude Code connect to external tools and data sources through standardized servers; adding them (e.g., claude mcp add or via config) exposes their tools and resources to the model. Pasting docs (1) gives static text, not live query access. Manual browser login (2) is not how Claude integrates data sources and is not reliable. Slash commands (3) are prompt templates, not a substitute for an MCP server's live tool/resource interface.
referenceText: Claude Code MCP integration

stem: An engineering team wants to build a custom autonomous coding agent — running in their own service, not the Claude Code CLI — that reuses Claude Code's agent loop, tool execution, and permission handling. Which offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK, which exposes the same agent harness for building custom agents programmatically
Copying the Claude Code binary and shelling out to it for every request
The prompt engineering guide's few-shot examples
A large CLAUDE.md that describes the desired agent behavior
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK provides the same underlying agent harness (loop, tool use, permissions, context management) that powers Claude Code, so teams can build custom agents in their own applications. Shelling out to the CLI (1) is fragile and not the supported programmatic surface. Few-shot examples (2) are a prompting technique, not an agent framework. A CLAUDE.md (3) only configures an existing Claude Code session and cannot host a custom service.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK overview

stem: A developer needs Claude to extract structured metadata (title, owners, severity) from free-text incident reports and return it so downstream code can parse it reliably without regex-scraping prose. What is the most reliable technique?
options: Define a tool with a JSON schema for the fields and have Claude call it, so the output conforms to the schema
Ask Claude in plain text to 'please return JSON' and hope the formatting is consistent
Lower the temperature to 0 and rely on the model to always emit valid JSON on its own
Put an example incident in CLAUDE.md and ask Claude to imitate it verbatim
rationale: Defining a tool (function) with a JSON schema and having the model call it constrains the output to the declared structure, which is the recommended path for reliable structured output. Simply asking for JSON in prose (1) yields inconsistent, sometimes malformed output. Temperature 0 (2) increases determinism but does not enforce a schema or guarantee valid JSON. A CLAUDE.md example (3) is weak conditioning and does not guarantee structure for varied inputs.
referenceText: Tool use for structured output

stem: Claude Code keeps generating commit messages in an inconsistent format even though the CLAUDE.md says 'write good commit messages.' Applying prompt-engineering best practice, what is the highest-leverage change?
options: Replace the vague instruction with explicit rules plus two or three concrete example messages showing the exact desired format
Repeat the phrase 'write good commit messages' several times for emphasis
Switch to a smaller, faster model to reduce variance
Increase temperature so the model explores more message styles
rationale: Vague directives like 'write good' underspecify the target; being explicit about the format and providing a few concrete examples (few-shot) is the core technique for consistent, on-spec output. Repetition (1) does not add specification. A smaller model (2) does not fix an underspecified prompt and may reduce quality. Higher temperature (3) increases variance, worsening consistency.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: be clear and direct

stem: An architect is defining a tool for Claude to call during automated refactors. Which practices improve the reliability of the tool-use interaction? (Select 2.)
options: Write a detailed tool description explaining what the tool does, when to use it, and what each parameter means
Specify a JSON schema for the input with typed, well-named parameters and mark required fields
Omit the description to keep the token count low and let the model infer usage
Rely on the tool name alone and give all parameters the type 'string' for flexibility
Ask the model to output the tool call as a Markdown code block instead of using the tool-use mechanism
rationale: Detailed tool descriptions (0) and precise, typed JSON-schema input definitions with required fields (1) are the most important factors for reliable tool use — the model chooses and fills tools based on these. Omitting the description (2) starves the model of the guidance it needs to call the tool correctly. Making everything a loosely-typed string (3) removes the structure that prevents malformed calls. Emitting a Markdown code block (4) bypasses the structured tool-use API and forfeits schema validation.
referenceText: Tool use best practices

stem: During a long generation task, Claude Code produces a plausible-looking config value but the developer suspects it was invented. Which prompting practice most directly reduces this kind of fabrication?
options: Instruct Claude to say it is unsure or to look up the value in the codebase rather than guess, and give it the tools to verify
Tell Claude to always sound confident so the output is easier to read
Ask the same question three times and take the majority answer
Raise the max output tokens so Claude has room to elaborate
rationale: Giving the model explicit permission to say 'I don't know' plus tools to verify (e.g., reading the file) is a standard technique to reduce hallucination in agentic settings. Encouraging confident tone (1) increases the risk of confident fabrication. Majority voting (2) does not help when the model consistently invents the same value and adds cost. More output tokens (3) gives room to elaborate but does nothing to ground the value in reality.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: reduce hallucinations

stem: Halfway through a large feature, a Claude Code session's context is cluttered with a completed, unrelated debugging tangent, and responses are drifting. The developer wants to keep working on the feature but shed the irrelevant history. What is the best action?
options: Use /clear to reset the conversation context, then re-establish only the feature's relevant context
Keep going and hope the model ignores the stale content
Delete the project's CLAUDE.md to free up context
Reduce the model's temperature to compensate for the noise
rationale: /clear resets the conversation so accumulated, irrelevant context stops degrading responses; the developer then reintroduces only what the current task needs. Continuing (1) lets stale context keep causing drift. Deleting CLAUDE.md (2) removes valuable persistent project guidance and does not address the polluted conversation. Temperature (3) does not remove irrelevant context.
referenceText: Claude Code managing context (/clear, /compact)

stem: A team runs Claude Code headlessly to triage failing tests overnight. They want it to attempt an automated fix, but if it cannot resolve an issue after a bounded effort, it must stop and record what it tried instead of thrashing indefinitely. Which design best supports this?
options: Define explicit stop conditions and an escalation path (e.g., after N attempts, write findings to a report file and exit) in the prompt/workflow
Instruct Claude to never give up until all tests pass
Remove all permissions so Claude cannot make changes at all
Run with maximum temperature so it tries more varied fixes each loop
rationale: Reliable agentic workflows need bounded effort, clear stop conditions, and an escalation path that captures structured state (what was tried) when the agent cannot succeed — exactly the described design. 'Never give up' (1) invites infinite loops and wasted spend. Removing all permissions (2) prevents the intended automated fix. Max temperature (3) adds randomness, not reliability or a stopping rule.
referenceText: Building reliable agents / stop conditions

stem: An architect is designing a multi-hour Claude Code migration that will exceed a single context window. She wants the agent to survive context compaction and resume without losing track of which files are done. What is the most robust pattern?
options: Have the agent maintain an external structured state file (e.g., a checklist/progress log) it reads and updates as it works
Rely on the model remembering everything in conversation history for the whole run
Disable /compact so the context never shrinks
Store progress only in the terminal scrollback for the developer to read
rationale: Persisting progress in an external structured state file (a checklist or log the agent reads/updates) makes long tasks resilient to context loss and compaction, letting the agent resume accurately. Relying on conversation memory (1) fails once context is compacted or exceeded. Disabling compaction (2) is not viable for tasks larger than the window and eventually breaks. Terminal scrollback (3) is not machine-readable state the agent can act on.
referenceText: Context management with structured state

stem: A developer reports a bug where an API returns 500 intermittently. She wants Claude Code to investigate methodically. Which prompting approach best fits Claude Code's debugging workflow?
options: Share the error message and stack trace, and ask Claude to reproduce, locate the cause, and propose a fix before editing
Tell Claude only 'the API is broken, fix it' and let it edit files immediately
Ask Claude to rewrite the entire service from scratch to avoid the bug
Paste the whole repository into a single prompt and ask for the answer
rationale: Effective debugging with Claude Code starts by giving it the concrete signal (error text, stack trace) and asking it to reproduce, isolate the root cause, and propose a fix — a targeted, evidence-driven loop. A vague 'it's broken' prompt (1) yields unfocused edits. A full rewrite (2) is disproportionate and risky. Dumping the entire repo (3) wastes context and buries the relevant signal; Claude can navigate the repo itself.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows (debugging)

stem: A security-conscious team runs Claude Code in CI and wants to restrict which tools it may use without a human approving each action, allowing only reading files and running the test command. Which mechanism is appropriate?
options: Configure allowed tools / permissions (e.g., permission settings or --allowedTools) so only the sanctioned actions run non-interactively
Trust plan mode to prevent unwanted edits in the automated run
Instruct Claude in the prompt to 'please do not delete anything'
Set a high temperature so Claude behaves more cautiously
rationale: Claude Code's permission system (settings and the allowed-tools configuration) is the supported way to constrain which tools run without prompting, which is essential for safe non-interactive CI. Plan mode (1) is interactive and not a CI permission control. A polite prompt instruction (2) is not an enforceable guardrail. Temperature (3) has no relationship to permissions or safety.
referenceText: Claude Code settings and permissions

stem: A staff engineer is deciding what belongs in the project CLAUDE.md versus a one-off prompt. Which items are appropriate, durable content for CLAUDE.md? (Select 2.)
options: The commands to build, test, and lint the project
Coding conventions and architectural patterns the team expects Claude to follow
The specific one-time task 'rename getUser to fetchUser in auth.ts today'
A copy of every source file's full contents so Claude never has to read them
Transient debug output from this afternoon's failing test run
rationale: CLAUDE.md is for durable, project-wide guidance that should always be in context: build/test/lint commands (0) and coding conventions/architecture (1). A single one-time task (2) belongs in a prompt, not persistent memory. Pasting every file's contents (3) bloats context and duplicates what Claude can read on demand. Transient debug output (4) is ephemeral and would just clutter memory.
referenceText: Claude Code memory best practices

stem: An architect wants a slash command that generates release notes and must always return them in a fixed sectioned template (Features, Fixes, Breaking Changes). To make the structure reliable across runs, what should the command's prompt include?
options: The exact template with headings plus one or two filled-in example release notes demonstrating the format
A single sentence: 'make nice release notes'
An instruction to be creative and vary the structure each time
A request to output the notes as an image
rationale: Providing the exact template and a couple of worked examples (few-shot) is the most effective way to lock in a consistent, sectioned output format. A vague one-liner (1) underspecifies the format and yields drift. Encouraging variation (2) directly undermines the fixed-template requirement. Requesting an image (3) is nonsensical for text release notes and unsupported here.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: use examples (multishot)

stem: When Claude Code calls a tool that fails (for example, a test command exits with an error), what is the recommended way to give the model a chance to recover within the agent loop?
options: Return the tool's error output back to the model as the tool result so it can read the failure and adjust its next step
Silently swallow the error and return an empty result so the model continues optimistically
Immediately terminate the session on any tool error with no information passed back
Convert the error into a success result so the loop keeps moving
rationale: Feeding the actual error output back as the tool result lets the model observe what went wrong and correct course, which is the core of resilient tool-use loops. Swallowing the error (1) hides the signal and leads the model to build on a false premise. Hard-terminating on any error (2) forfeits recovery for transient or fixable failures. Faking success (3) corrupts the agent's world model and guarantees downstream mistakes.
referenceText: Tool use: handling tool result errors

stem: A team lead sets shared coding standards in the repository's ./CLAUDE.md, while an individual engineer keeps personal shortcuts in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. On the engineer's machine the two files give conflicting guidance about test-runner commands. When the engineer runs Claude Code inside the repo, how does Claude Code resolve which memory applies?
options: Both files are loaded and combined into context; where guidance conflicts, project memory takes precedence over the user's personal memory.
Only the project ./CLAUDE.md is loaded; the user-level file is ignored entirely whenever a repository CLAUDE.md exists.
Only the user ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is loaded, because personal memory always overrides project memory.
Neither file is loaded automatically; the engineer must import each one with an explicit @path reference at session start.
rationale: Claude Code loads all applicable memory files (enterprise, project, then user) and combines them, with higher-level (enterprise/project) memory taking precedence over user memory when guidance conflicts, so the shared project standard wins. It is wrong that the user file is ignored or that only one file loads: both are read. It is wrong that user memory overrides project memory: the precedence runs the other way. Memory files load automatically by discovery, so no manual @path import is required.
referenceText: Claude Code reads memories recursively; memory types have precedence with enterprise and project memory taking precedence over user memory.

stem: A platform team maintains a canonical set of API conventions in docs/api-guidelines.md and wants every service repo's CLAUDE.md to pull in that guidance without copying the text into each file. What is the intended Claude Code mechanism?
options: Add an import line such as @docs/api-guidelines.md inside CLAUDE.md so its contents are pulled in when memory loads.
Rename docs/api-guidelines.md to CLAUDE.local.md so Claude Code auto-discovers it as an additional memory file.
Paste the full guidelines into a fenced code block in CLAUDE.md so they are treated as authoritative instructions.
Register the guidelines file as an MCP resource so Claude fetches it on demand during each turn.
rationale: CLAUDE.md supports importing other files with @path syntax, letting shared content live in one place and be pulled into memory. Renaming to CLAUDE.local.md does not import another file and that pattern is deprecated in favor of imports. Pasting the full text defeats the single-source-of-truth goal and imports inside code spans are explicitly not evaluated. An MCP resource is a different mechanism for tool/data access, not for composing memory files.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files can import additional files using @path/to/import syntax; imports are not evaluated inside code spans.

stem: An engineer opens Claude Code in a large existing repository that has no CLAUDE.md yet and wants Claude to generate an initial project-memory file summarizing structure, build commands, and conventions. Which action is designed for this?
options: Run the /init slash command to bootstrap a CLAUDE.md from the codebase.
Run the /compact slash command to distill the repository into memory.
Type the whole repository tree into the prompt and ask Claude to remember it.
Set output-format to json so Claude writes memory during the next headless run.
rationale: /init is the built-in command that scans the project and scaffolds a CLAUDE.md with structure, commands, and conventions. /compact summarizes the current conversation context, not the repo, and does not create a memory file. Manually pasting the tree is unnecessary and error-prone. --output-format json controls how programmatic output is serialized in headless mode and has nothing to do with generating memory.
referenceText: Use /init to bootstrap a CLAUDE.md for your codebase.

stem: Mid-session, an engineer discovers a non-obvious rule ("always run migrations before seeding") that Claude keeps forgetting across turns. They want to persist it to memory quickly without leaving the session or manually opening a file. What is the fastest supported way?
options: Begin a line of input with # and type the rule; Claude Code prompts to store it in a memory file.
Wrap the rule in triple backticks so Claude Code auto-appends it to CLAUDE.md.
Prefix the rule with ! so Claude Code executes and then remembers it.
Send /clear followed by the rule so it becomes part of the compacted summary.
rationale: Starting an input with # is the built-in shortcut to add a quick memory; Claude Code asks which memory file to store it in. Triple backticks denote a code span and are explicitly not treated as memory. The ! prefix is for running shell commands, not saving memory. /clear wipes conversation context and would discard the rule rather than persist it.
referenceText: Quickly add a memory by starting your input with the # character; you'll be prompted to select which memory file to store it in.

stem: A team wants a reusable /fix-issue command that takes an issue number and injects a standard triage prompt. They create .claude/commands/fix-issue.md. Which two statements about authoring this custom slash command are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Use the $ARGUMENTS placeholder in the file so the text after the command name is substituted into the prompt.
Placing the file under .claude/commands/ makes it a project command available to everyone who clones the repo.
The command must be written in JSON with a top-level "prompt" key or Claude Code will not register it.
Custom commands can only run built-in read-only tools and cannot be given a description or argument hint.
The file name must exactly match an existing built-in command to override its behavior.
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files whose body becomes the prompt, and $ARGUMENTS captures everything typed after the command name. Files under the project's .claude/commands/ directory are project-scoped and shared via source control. Commands are Markdown, not JSON. They support optional frontmatter such as description, argument-hint, and allowed-tools, and can be granted tools, so the read-only/no-metadata claim is false. Names do not need to match built-ins.
referenceText: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands (project) or ~/.claude/commands (personal); $ARGUMENTS captures passed arguments; frontmatter supports description, argument-hint, and allowed-tools.

stem: An engineer writes a custom command that should first show the current git status inline in the prompt, then ask Claude to summarize uncommitted work. They want the git command to actually run and its output embedded when the command is invoked. What must the command file include?
options: A line beginning with ! to execute the shell command, plus allowed-tools frontmatter granting the Bash tool.
A line beginning with @ referencing git so Claude Code shells out automatically at load time.
A tool_choice: bash frontmatter field, which auto-approves any shell command in the body.
Nothing special; any backticked shell snippet in a command body is executed before the prompt is sent.
rationale: In a slash command, a line prefixed with ! executes a bash command and embeds its output, but this requires allowed-tools frontmatter that includes the Bash tool (optionally constrained to specific commands). The @ prefix references file contents, not shell execution. There is no tool_choice: bash frontmatter field in Claude Code commands. Backticked snippets are inert text, not executed.
referenceText: Commands can execute bash before the prompt runs using the ! prefix, and must include the Bash tool via allowed-tools frontmatter; @ is used to reference files.

stem: One engineer keeps a personal /scratch command that only makes sense for their own workflow and should never appear for teammates or be committed. Where should the command file live?
options: ~/.claude/commands/scratch.md, which registers it as a personal command scoped to that user across all projects.
The repository's .claude/commands/scratch.md, marked private with a frontmatter visibility: personal field.
The repository root as scratch.CLAUDE.md so it is treated as a per-user memory rather than a shared command.
.claude/commands/scratch.local.md, which Claude Code hides from git but shares within the team.
rationale: Personal commands live in ~/.claude/commands/ and are available to that user across every project without being part of any repo. Putting the file in the repo's .claude/commands/ makes it a project command shared with the team, and there is no visibility: personal frontmatter that changes that. The *.CLAUDE.md and *.local.md naming schemes for commands are fabricated. Scope is determined by directory, not a magic filename.
referenceText: Commands in ~/.claude/commands are personal (available in all your projects); commands in a project's .claude/commands are project commands shared with the team.

stem: An engineer must refactor authentication logic spread across an unfamiliar service and is worried Claude will start editing files before the approach is understood. They want Claude to investigate and propose a step-by-step plan first, making no changes until approved. Which Claude Code capability fits?
options: Plan mode, which restricts Claude to read-only analysis and presents a plan for approval before any edits.
Auto-accept edits mode, so approved edits are applied immediately as Claude reasons through the code.
Headless mode with --output-format json, so the plan is emitted as machine-readable output.
The /compact command, which trims context so Claude can hold the whole refactor in one pass.
rationale: Plan mode constrains Claude to read-only exploration and produces a plan you approve before execution, which is exactly the safe-investigation workflow described. Auto-accept edits mode does the opposite by applying changes without a pause. Headless JSON output governs serialization for scripting, not the plan-first safety guarantee. /compact only summarizes conversation context and does not gate edits.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude research and produce a plan without modifying files; you review and approve before it executes.

stem: A reviewer claims plan mode is unsafe because Claude might still write to disk while exploring. Which statement most accurately characterizes plan mode's guarantee?
options: While in plan mode, Claude Code is limited to read-only operations and does not make edits or run commands that mutate the workspace.
Plan mode allows edits but automatically reverts them with git if the plan is rejected.
Plan mode disables only network access, while file writes proceed normally.
Plan mode is identical to normal mode except that it hides Claude's reasoning until the end.
rationale: Plan mode is intentionally read-only: Claude gathers information and drafts a plan without mutating the workspace, so the reviewer's concern is unfounded. It does not permit edits-then-revert; it prevents the edits in the first place. It is not merely a network toggle, and it is not normal mode with hidden reasoning. The whole point is a no-write investigation phase.
referenceText: Plan mode restricts Claude to read-only actions so it can analyze and plan without changing files.

stem: During an interactive session an engineer wants to switch from the default behavior into plan mode and later into auto-accept-edits without retyping their request. What is the intended way to move between these permission/interaction modes?
options: Press Shift+Tab to cycle through the available modes (including plan mode and auto-accept edits) during the session.
Restart Claude Code each time with a different --mode flag, since modes cannot change mid-session.
Edit CLAUDE.md to set a mode: field, which takes effect on the next turn.
Run /model to select a mode, since modes are tied to the underlying model choice.
rationale: Shift+Tab cycles through interaction modes such as plan mode and auto-accept edits without leaving the session. Modes are switchable live, so a restart with a flag is unnecessary. There is no mode: field in CLAUDE.md that governs this. /model selects the model, not the interaction mode.
referenceText: Use Shift+Tab to cycle interaction modes, including plan mode and auto-accept edits.

stem: A CI job needs Claude Code to generate a changelog from a diff and exit, with no interactive prompt and no waiting for human input. Which invocation is designed for this non-interactive, scriptable use?
options: Run claude -p "<prompt>" to execute a single request in headless (print) mode and exit.
Run claude and pipe "yes" into stdin so the interactive REPL auto-confirms each step.
Run claude --interactive with a background flag so the REPL detaches from the terminal.
Run claude /init in the pipeline, since project bootstrapping is the only supported CI entry point.
rationale: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code headlessly for a single prompt and exits, which is the supported pattern for CI and scripting. Piping "yes" into the interactive REPL is brittle and not the intended mechanism. There is no --interactive background mode for detaching the REPL. /init bootstraps memory and is unrelated to running arbitrary CI tasks.
referenceText: Use claude -p (print mode) to run Claude Code headlessly for non-interactive and CI use.

stem: A pipeline runs Claude Code headlessly and a downstream script must reliably parse the result, including intermediate events for a live log. Which two flag choices support this? (Select 2.)
options: Pass --output-format json to receive a single structured result object suitable for parsing.
Pass --output-format stream-json to receive newline-delimited JSON events as they occur.
Pass --output-format markdown, which guarantees a machine-parseable schema for each turn.
Rely on the default text output and regex the human-readable transcript for fields.
Pass --raw so the script receives the model's raw tokens without any envelope.
rationale: --output-format json emits a structured result object for reliable parsing, and --output-format stream-json emits incremental JSON events, which is what a live log needs. There is no markdown output format that guarantees a schema. Regexing default text output is exactly the fragile approach structured output is meant to replace. --raw is not a Claude Code output format.
referenceText: --output-format supports text, json, and stream-json; json returns a structured result and stream-json emits events incrementally.

stem: A team wants everyone who clones their repo to automatically get the same MCP server (an internal issue tracker) configured in Claude Code, checked into source control. Which configuration achieves shared, project-scoped MCP setup?
options: Commit a .mcp.json file at the project root defining the server, which Claude Code loads as project-scoped MCP config.
Have each developer add the server with a --local flag so it is stored per-machine outside the repo.
Place the server definition in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md so it loads as user memory on every machine.
Define the server inside a slash command file so it is registered whenever the command runs.
rationale: A checked-in .mcp.json at the project root is the project-scoped mechanism that shares MCP servers with everyone who clones the repo. A --local/local scope is deliberately per-user and not shared. CLAUDE.md is memory/instructions, not MCP server configuration. Slash command files are prompts, not MCP registration.
referenceText: Project-scoped MCP servers are configured in a .mcp.json file checked into the repository and shared with the team.

stem: An architect is explaining why Claude Code uses the Model Context Protocol rather than hardcoding each integration. Which statement best captures MCP's role?
options: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLMs, so Claude Code can connect to many servers through one interface.
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic API that only Claude models can call, replacing standard tool use.
MCP is a file format for storing conversation memory between Claude Code sessions.
MCP is a linter protocol that validates generated code before it is written to disk.
rationale: MCP is an open standard for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to LLM applications, letting a client like Claude Code integrate many servers uniformly. It is not proprietary or Claude-only; it is an open protocol usable by many clients. It is not a memory file format nor a linting protocol. Those descriptions confuse MCP with unrelated features.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context (tools, resources, prompts) to LLMs.

stem: A developer is building a helper that must return classification results as strict JSON (label plus confidence) that a program consumes directly. They want to make it hard for the model to reply with prose. Using the Claude API tool-use pattern, what is the most reliable approach?
options: Define a tool with an input_schema for the JSON shape and force it via tool_choice so the model must return arguments matching the schema.
Ask in the prompt for JSON only and increase temperature so responses vary less.
Instruct the model to wrap the JSON in a Markdown code fence and post-process with a regex.
Send the request without a system prompt so the model defaults to structured output.
rationale: Defining a tool with an input_schema and forcing it with tool_choice makes the model emit arguments conforming to your JSON schema, which is the reliable structured-output pattern. Prompt-only JSON requests are less reliable, and raising temperature increases variability rather than reducing it. Regex-scraping fenced JSON is exactly the brittleness tool use avoids. Omitting the system prompt does not induce structured output.
referenceText: Tool use with a defined input_schema and tool_choice can force Claude to produce structured JSON output matching your schema.

stem: Claude keeps generating commit messages in an inconsistent style despite a written description of the desired format. The engineer wants the most effective prompt-engineering technique to lock in the style. What should they add?
options: A few concrete input/output examples (multishot examples) demonstrating the exact desired commit-message format.
A stern instruction that Claude must never deviate from the format under any circumstances.
A higher max_tokens value so Claude has room to self-correct the format.
A request for Claude to explain its formatting choices after each message.
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) examples are the highest-leverage way to pin down an exact output style, because the model imitates demonstrated patterns more reliably than described ones. Absolute never-deviate wording does not improve consistency and reads as an unhelpful distractor. Raising max_tokens affects length budget, not format adherence. Asking for post-hoc explanations adds noise without fixing the style.
referenceText: Multishot prompting: including several well-chosen examples significantly improves the consistency and accuracy of Claude's output format.

stem: An engineer is debugging a failing test with Claude Code and wants to maximize the chance Claude fixes the real root cause rather than guessing. Which two practices best support reliable debugging? (Select 2.)
options: Paste the exact failing test output and stack trace so Claude reasons from concrete evidence.
Let Claude run the test suite so it can observe the failure and verify its fix against real results.
Tell Claude the answer is 'probably a typo somewhere' and ask it to skim the file quickly.
Ask Claude to apply a fix and immediately commit without re-running the tests to save time.
Disable the failing test so the suite passes and the pipeline unblocks.
rationale: Giving Claude the exact error output and letting it run the tests to observe and verify grounds the fix in real behavior, which is the reliable debugging loop. Vague hints like 'probably a typo' bias Claude toward guessing. Committing without re-running tests skips verification and risks shipping a non-fix. Disabling the test hides the defect rather than resolving it.
referenceText: Effective debugging with Claude Code: provide the error/stack trace and let Claude run tests to reproduce and verify fixes.

stem: After a long Claude Code session spanning several unrelated tasks, responses are getting slower and less focused as the context fills. The engineer is about to start a brand-new, unrelated task. What is the most appropriate context-management action?
options: Run /clear to reset the conversation context before starting the unrelated task.
Run /compact after every single message to keep the context permanently minimal.
Delete CLAUDE.md so project memory stops consuming the context window.
Lower max_tokens so each response leaves more room in the context window.
rationale: /clear resets conversation history, which is ideal when moving to an unrelated task so stale context stops degrading focus and speed. /compact summarizes rather than clears and running it after every message is wasteful and not the right tool when you want a fresh start. Deleting CLAUDE.md throws away useful project guidance to solve the wrong problem. Lowering max_tokens caps output length, not accumulated conversation context.
referenceText: /clear resets the conversation context; /compact summarizes the conversation to reduce context usage.

stem: A complex task requires searching a large monorepo for all usages of a deprecated API, which would flood the main conversation with file contents and noise. The engineer wants that exploration isolated so it does not bloat the primary context. Which Claude Code feature is designed for this?
options: Delegate the search to a subagent, which runs with its own context and returns only a concise result to the main thread.
Increase the main session's max context and paste every file in so nothing is missed.
Switch to headless mode mid-session so intermediate output is discarded automatically.
Use /init to regenerate CLAUDE.md so the deprecated API is documented instead of searched.
rationale: Subagents run with their own isolated context and report back a summarized result, which keeps heavy exploration from polluting the main conversation. Manually pasting every file does the opposite and bloats context. Headless mode is a non-interactive run configuration, not a mid-session context-isolation switch. /init bootstraps memory and does not perform or isolate the search.
referenceText: Subagents operate in a separate context window and return results to the main agent, preserving the primary conversation's context.

stem: A team wants a linter to run automatically every time Claude Code edits a file, deterministically and without relying on Claude to remember to do it. Which Claude Code mechanism enforces this?
options: Configure a PostToolUse hook that runs the linter after file-editing tools execute.
Add 'always run the linter after edits' to CLAUDE.md and trust Claude to comply each time.
Create a slash command the engineer must remember to invoke after every change.
Set --output-format json so lint results are appended to each response automatically.
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically at defined lifecycle points; a PostToolUse hook fires after tool execution (e.g., edits) and can run a linter regardless of what Claude decides. Putting the instruction in CLAUDE.md relies on the model choosing to comply, which is not deterministic. A manual slash command depends on the human remembering. Output format controls serialization and does not run a linter.
referenceText: Hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute deterministically at lifecycle events such as PostToolUse, e.g., to run formatters or linters after edits.

stem: A team keeps re-typing the same instructions to Claude Code every session: use pnpm not npm, run tests with `pnpm test`, and never edit files under `generated/`. They want Claude to automatically honor these rules in every session for this repository without pasting them each time. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md file at the repository root describing the conventions so it is auto-loaded into context each session
Paste the rules into a custom system prompt via the --append-system-prompt flag on every invocation
Create a slash command named /rules that prints the conventions when the user runs it
Add the rules as comments at the top of each source file so Claude reads them when it opens files
rationale: CLAUDE.md at the project root is automatically pulled into context at the start of every session and is the canonical place for persistent project conventions, and committing it shares it with the whole team. --append-system-prompt must be supplied on each run and is not persisted per-repo. A /rules slash command only helps if someone remembers to invoke it. Per-file comments are read only when that file is opened and do not establish repo-wide rules.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: CLAUDE.md is loaded automatically at session start and is the recommended place for project conventions.

stem: An engineer wants Claude Code to research a large refactor, propose a step-by-step approach, and get their sign-off BEFORE any files are modified. Which Claude Code capability directly enforces this read-only investigate-then-approve workflow?
options: Plan mode, which restricts Claude to read-only analysis and presents a plan for approval before execution
Running Claude Code with the --dangerously-skip-permissions flag
Adding a CLAUDE.md line that says 'always ask before editing'
Using a /commit slash command to gate changes behind version control
rationale: Plan mode constrains Claude to read/analyze the codebase without making edits, then surfaces a plan the user must approve before Claude switches to execution. --dangerously-skip-permissions does the opposite by removing approval prompts. A CLAUDE.md instruction is a soft guideline that Claude may not enforce reliably as a hard gate. A /commit command runs after changes are already made, so it does not prevent premature edits.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows: plan mode lets Claude investigate and propose a plan before making changes.

stem: A developer creates a file at `.claude/commands/review-pr.md` containing a prompt template with the line 'Review the diff and focus on $ARGUMENTS'. In a session they type `/review-pr security`. What happens?
options: Claude runs the review-pr command with 'security' substituted for $ARGUMENTS in the template
Claude ignores the file because slash commands must be defined in settings.json
Claude treats 'security' as a filename to open before running the command
The command fails because $ARGUMENTS is only valid in CLAUDE.md, not in command files
rationale: A Markdown file in .claude/commands defines a project-scoped custom slash command; its filename becomes the command name and $ARGUMENTS is replaced by whatever text follows the invocation. Slash commands are defined by these Markdown files, not settings.json. The argument text is a literal string passed into the prompt, not a filename. $ARGUMENTS is specifically a slash-command placeholder, not a CLAUDE.md feature.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: custom commands live in .claude/commands as Markdown files and support the $ARGUMENTS placeholder.

stem: A CI pipeline needs Claude Code to run non-interactively on each pull request, produce a fixed-format JSON summary of lint findings, and exit so the result can be parsed by a downstream script. Which invocation approach best fits?
options: Run Claude Code in headless mode with the -p (print) flag and request JSON output for machine parsing
Launch the interactive TUI and screen-scrape the terminal output
Use plan mode so the pipeline can approve each step
Configure a CLAUDE.md that tells Claude to open a browser and display results
rationale: Headless mode (`claude -p`) runs a single non-interactive prompt and can emit structured/JSON output suited for CI parsing, then exits. The interactive TUI is designed for humans and is not scriptable via scraping. Plan mode requires an interactive approver, which does not exist in an unattended pipeline. A browser display is meaningless in CI.
referenceText: Claude Code headless mode: use -p for non-interactive runs and structured output in automation and CI.

stem: A team wants their CLAUDE.md to be maximally effective at steering Claude Code across many sessions. Which practices improve its usefulness? (Select 2.)
options: Keep it concise and specific, listing concrete commands and conventions rather than vague prose
Use it to document only the most important, frequently needed project context and keep it current
Paste the entire contents of every source file into it so Claude has the full codebase in memory
Store secrets like API keys in it so Claude can authenticate to services automatically
Fill it with speculative future plans and unrelated company history for completeness
rationale: CLAUDE.md works best when concise, specific, and kept up to date with the conventions and commands Claude actually needs; every token there is loaded each session. Dumping entire source files wastes the context window and duplicates what Claude can read on demand. Storing secrets in a committed file is a serious security risk. Padding with speculative or irrelevant content dilutes the signal and degrades steering.
referenceText: Claude Code memory best practices: keep CLAUDE.md concise, specific, and current; do not store secrets.

stem: A monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md with general rules, and a `services/payments/CLAUDE.md` with rules specific to the payments service. Claude Code is working on a file inside `services/payments/`. How are these memory files applied?
options: Both are loaded, with the more specific payments CLAUDE.md layered on top of the root file
Only the root CLAUDE.md is used; nested memory files are ignored
Only the payments CLAUDE.md is used; the root file is discarded when a nested one exists
The two files are concatenated randomly, so ordering is nondeterministic
rationale: Claude Code supports nested/hierarchical memory: it reads the root CLAUDE.md plus more specific CLAUDE.md files closer to the working files, layering the specific context on top of the general context. Nested files are not ignored, nor do they wholly replace the root. The layering is deterministic (general then specific), not random.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: supports project-level and nested CLAUDE.md files that combine hierarchically.

stem: A developer wants Claude Code to connect to their company's internal ticketing system so Claude can read ticket details while coding. The ticketing vendor publishes an MCP server. What is the correct integration path?
options: Add the vendor's MCP server to Claude Code's MCP configuration so its tools/resources become available to Claude
Rewrite the ticketing API as a set of custom slash commands in .claude/commands
Paste each ticket's text into CLAUDE.md before every session
Ask Claude to guess ticket contents from commit messages since external systems cannot be integrated
rationale: MCP is the standard for connecting Claude Code to external tools and data sources; registering the vendor's MCP server exposes its tools and resources to Claude. Slash commands are prompt templates, not a protocol for live external data. Pasting tickets into CLAUDE.md is manual and does not scale. External systems absolutely can be integrated via MCP, so guessing is unnecessary and wrong.
referenceText: Claude Code MCP: connect external tools and data sources by configuring MCP servers.

stem: During a long debugging session, Claude Code's context is filling up with the output of many exploratory commands, and responses are starting to lose track of the original goal. The user wants to preserve the essential findings while freeing context. Which action best addresses this?
options: Use /compact to summarize and condense the conversation while retaining key findings
Immediately run /clear to wipe all context and start over from scratch
Increase the model's temperature so it pays more attention to recent messages
Add more detail to CLAUDE.md mid-session to override the accumulated context
rationale: /compact condenses the conversation into a summary, reclaiming context window space while retaining the important state and findings, which is exactly what a long session needs. /clear discards everything including the findings the user wants to keep. Temperature does not manage context length or focus. Editing CLAUDE.md does not remove the already-accumulated transcript from the window.
referenceText: Claude Code: /compact summarizes the conversation to reduce context usage while preserving key information.

stem: A staff engineer wants Claude Code to automatically run `pnpm lint --fix` and block the edit if linting fails every time Claude writes a file, without relying on Claude choosing to do so. Which Claude Code feature is designed for this deterministic automation?
options: A PostToolUse hook configured to run the linter after file-editing tools
A carefully worded CLAUDE.md instruction asking Claude to always lint
A /lint slash command the user runs manually after each change
Plan mode, which lints every proposed step before execution
rationale: Hooks are user-defined shell commands the harness executes deterministically at lifecycle events; a PostToolUse hook runs after tool calls like file edits and can enforce linting regardless of the model's choices. CLAUDE.md is a soft instruction the model may skip. A manual slash command depends on the user remembering. Plan mode governs approval of a plan, not automatic post-edit shell execution.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks: shell commands run deterministically at events such as PostToolUse.

stem: A user asks Claude Code to add a feature that requires touching 30 files across an unfamiliar codebase. To reduce the risk of Claude going off-track and to keep the work reviewable, what is the recommended approach?
options: Have Claude first produce a plan in plan mode, review and refine it, then execute in reviewable increments
Tell Claude to make all 30 edits in a single turn without stopping so it stays consistent
Run with --dangerously-skip-permissions to avoid interruptions during the large change
Delete the existing tests first so they do not block the new edits
rationale: For large, unfamiliar changes, using plan mode to align on an approach and then executing in reviewable increments keeps the work on track and easy to review. Forcing all edits in one uninterrupted turn removes checkpoints and makes errors harder to catch. Skipping permissions increases risk on a large change. Deleting tests removes the safety net that would catch regressions.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows: use plan mode for complex changes and work in reviewable steps.

stem: A developer wants a reusable custom slash command that any teammate gets automatically when they clone the repo, versus one that only exists on their own machine across all projects. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two scopes?
options: Project commands live in `.claude/commands/` in the repo and are shared via git; personal commands live in `~/.claude/commands/` and are available across all of that user's projects
Project commands live in `~/.claude/commands/`; personal commands live in `.claude/commands/` inside the repo
Both project and personal commands must be registered in the global settings.json to work
Slash commands cannot be shared through version control and are always machine-local
rationale: Project-scoped commands are stored in the repo's .claude/commands/ directory and travel with the repository through git, while personal (user) commands live under ~/.claude/commands/ and apply across all the user's projects. The two directories are not swapped. Commands are defined as Markdown files, not registered in settings.json. They can absolutely be shared via version control when placed in the project directory.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: project commands in .claude/commands are shared via git; personal commands in ~/.claude/commands apply across projects.

stem: Claude Code is asked to fix a failing test but the test failure is caused by a flaky external network call. On the first run the call times out. What is the most reliable way for the user to help Claude reason about and handle this correctly?
options: Share the actual error output and ask Claude to distinguish a genuine bug from an environmental/flaky failure before changing code
Instruct Claude to keep re-running the test in a loop until it passes and then stop
Ask Claude to delete the failing assertion so the suite goes green
Tell Claude to add a very long sleep before every test to mask timing issues
rationale: Giving Claude the real error output and asking it to classify the failure (genuine defect vs. flaky environmental issue) lets it choose an appropriate fix such as adding a retry or mocking the network, rather than corrupting the logic. Blindly re-running until green hides nondeterminism without fixing it. Deleting the assertion removes coverage and masks the bug. A blanket long sleep degrades the suite and does not address the root cause.
referenceText: Claude Code debugging workflows: provide error output and let Claude diagnose root cause before editing.

stem: A platform team is building a headless Claude Code job for CI that must be robust and safe. Which practices are appropriate for this automated, non-interactive context? (Select 2.)
options: Scope tool permissions with an allowlist so the job can only run the commands it needs
Capture and parse the structured (JSON) output to drive downstream pipeline steps
Enable an interactive approval prompt so a human confirms each action during the CI run
Grant --dangerously-skip-permissions broadly and give the job unrestricted shell access
Rely on the model to invent credentials at runtime instead of providing them
rationale: In CI, constraining the tool permission allowlist limits blast radius, and consuming structured JSON output lets subsequent pipeline stages act on results deterministically. Interactive approval prompts cannot be satisfied in an unattended pipeline and would hang the job. Broadly skipping permissions with unrestricted shell access is a major security risk. Models cannot conjure valid credentials; secrets must be supplied securely via the environment.
referenceText: Claude Code headless/CI: use permission allowlists and structured output; avoid interactive prompts in automation.

stem: A user has been iterating in an interactive session and now wants to hand the current task to a fresh Claude Code session because the conversation has drifted far from the original request and accumulated dead ends. Which command starts a clean context in the current project?
options: /clear, which resets the conversation context for a fresh start
/compact, which keeps the full transcript intact and only reformats it
/init, which regenerates the codebase but keeps the conversation
/rewind, which replays every prior message into the new context
rationale: /clear resets the conversation context, giving a clean slate while staying in the same project, which is what the user wants after accumulating dead ends. /compact summarizes rather than fully clearing, so drift and clutter can persist. /init is for scaffolding a CLAUDE.md, not resetting a conversation. A /rewind that replays all prior messages would reintroduce the very clutter the user is trying to escape.
referenceText: Claude Code CLI: /clear resets the current conversation context.

stem: A large documentation-generation task requires Claude Code to summarize dozens of modules and then write a cohesive architecture overview. To keep the main session focused and avoid polluting its context with every file's raw contents, what is the recommended pattern?
options: Delegate the per-module summarization to subagents whose detailed context stays isolated, returning only concise summaries to the main session
Load every module's full source into the main context at once so nothing is missed
Disable plan mode so Claude writes documentation faster
Store each module's full text in CLAUDE.md so it is always in memory
rationale: Subagents run in their own context windows, so delegating per-module summarization keeps the bulky raw reading isolated and returns only distilled summaries to the orchestrating session, preserving its focus. Loading all sources into the main context risks overflow and dilution. Plan mode is unrelated to context isolation. Putting full module text in CLAUDE.md permanently bloats every session's memory.
referenceText: Claude Code subagents: run in separate context windows and return focused results to the main session.

stem: An architect is comparing Claude Code (the interactive/CLI product) with the Claude Agent SDK. A colleague wants to embed Claude's agentic coding loop inside their own internal web application with custom UI and orchestration. Which choice is most appropriate and why?
options: Use the Claude Agent SDK, which exposes the agent harness (tools, context management, loop) for building custom agent applications
Use the interactive Claude Code TUI embedded in an iframe as the app's backend
Reimplement the agent loop from scratch because no supported building block exists
Use CLAUDE.md as the integration API between the web app and Claude
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK provides the same agent harness that powers Claude Code (tool use, context management, the agentic loop) as building blocks for developers embedding agents in their own applications. Embedding the human-facing TUI in an iframe is not a supported programmatic backend. Reimplementing from scratch ignores the SDK that exists for this purpose. CLAUDE.md is a memory file, not an integration API.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK: build custom agents on the same harness that powers Claude Code.

stem: A refactoring task asks Claude Code to rename a widely used function and update all call sites. Partway through, Claude realizes the change also breaks a public API that external consumers depend on. According to reliability best practices, what is the appropriate behavior?
options: Pause and escalate to the user, surfacing the public-API impact and asking how to proceed rather than silently changing the contract
Silently rename the public API too and continue, since the task said to update all call sites
Abandon the entire task without reporting why
Delete the external consumers' code paths so nothing breaks internally
rationale: When a change exceeds the safe scope of the original request, such as altering a public API contract, the reliable behavior is to pause and escalate to the user with the impact and options, letting a human decide. Silently breaking a public contract violates the spirit of the request and surprises external consumers. Abandoning without explanation strands the user. Deleting external consumers' code paths is destructive and out of scope.
referenceText: Claude agent reliability: escalate to a human when an action exceeds intended scope or has broad impact.

stem: A developer wants Claude Code, when run headless in a script, to return its answer as JSON that always conforms to a fixed shape (fields: summary, files_changed, risk_level) so the wrapper program never has to parse free-form prose. Beyond requesting JSON output mode, which prompt-engineering practice most improves conformance?
options: Specify the exact schema and provide a concrete example of the desired JSON in the prompt
Ask for the answer in Markdown and hope the script can extract fields with regex
Raise temperature to encourage more varied field names
Tell the model to be creative about which fields to include
rationale: Explicitly stating the required schema and giving a concrete example (few-shot) strongly steers the model toward consistent, machine-parseable output. Requesting Markdown and regex-scraping is brittle and the opposite of structured output. Higher temperature increases variability, hurting conformance. Encouraging creativity about fields guarantees the schema will drift, which defeats the goal.
referenceText: Prompt engineering for structured output: specify the schema and include an example to increase JSON conformance.

stem: A team wants a custom slash command `/fix-issue` that pulls the current issue number from an argument, reads the issue via their GitHub MCP tools, and drafts a fix. Where should the reusable command definition live, and what should it reference?
options: A Markdown file in .claude/commands/fix-issue.md whose prompt uses $ARGUMENTS and instructs Claude to use the available GitHub MCP tools
A JSON block inside settings.json under a 'commands' key that hardcodes issue #1
A hook in .claude/hooks that fires on every keystroke
An entry in CLAUDE.md that is only read when the user pastes the issue body manually
rationale: Custom commands are Markdown prompt templates in .claude/commands; the file can reference $ARGUMENTS for the issue number and instruct Claude to use whatever MCP tools are configured (like GitHub) to read the issue. Slash commands are not defined as JSON in settings.json, and hardcoding one issue defeats reuse. A per-keystroke hook is unrelated to on-demand issue fixing. A CLAUDE.md note is not an invokable command and here would require manual pasting.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: Markdown templates that can invoke available tools and take $ARGUMENTS.

stem: A user notices Claude Code repeatedly asks for permission before running `git status` and `pnpm test`, which are safe and frequent in their workflow. They want to stop the prompts for these specific commands without disabling permissions entirely. What is the correct approach?
options: Add those commands to the allowlist in the project's .claude/settings.json permissions configuration
Run every session with --dangerously-skip-permissions to remove all prompts
Move the commands into CLAUDE.md so Claude treats them as pre-approved
Rename the commands inside a slash command so the permission system cannot see them
rationale: The permissions configuration in settings.json lets you allowlist specific commands so they run without prompting while everything else still requires approval, which precisely matches the request. --dangerously-skip-permissions removes all safety prompts, far broader than intended. CLAUDE.md is memory, not a permissions grant. Hiding commands inside a slash command does not and should not bypass the permission model.
referenceText: Claude Code settings: configure a permissions allowlist to pre-approve specific tools/commands.

stem: In the multi-agent research system, the coordinator frequently performs web research itself in the main thread instead of delegating to the web-search subagent. Which change most directly improves delegation?
options: Write a clear, action-oriented `description` on the web-search subagent stating exactly when it should be used
Grant the web-search subagent access to every tool the coordinator can use
Lower the coordinator model's temperature so it is more decisive
Rename the subagent definition file so it sorts first alphabetically
rationale: Claude selects a subagent by matching the task to the subagent's `description`; a vague description means the coordinator handles the task itself. Broad tool access does not affect delegation routing. Temperature changes token sampling, not delegation logic. File-name ordering is not how subagents are selected.
referenceText: Claude Code subagents: Claude uses each subagent's description to decide when to delegate.

stem: The document-analysis stage must process 40 large PDFs. Why is routing this to a dedicated subagent preferable to analyzing them directly in the coordinator's conversation?
options: Each subagent runs in its own context window and returns only a distilled summary, keeping the coordinator's context uncluttered
Subagents bypass the model's maximum context window limit entirely
Subagents are billed at a flat rate regardless of tokens processed
Only subagents are permitted to read files from disk
rationale: Subagents preserve the coordinator's context by doing bulky work in a separate window and returning just the summary. Each subagent still has a finite context window (option 1 is false). Billing is token-based, not flat. File access is governed by tool permissions, not by being a subagent.
referenceText: Subagents: each runs in its own context window and returns only the summary.

stem: The web-search subagent runs many cheap, repetitive fetch-and-extract calls that dominate cost. Which lever reduces cost without altering the coordinator's behavior?
options: Set the web-search subagent's `model` to a faster, cheaper model such as Haiku
Set `max_tokens` to 1 on the coordinator so it emits less output
Remove all tools from the web-search subagent so it stops calling APIs
Move the subagent's configuration into the project CLAUDE.md file
rationale: A subagent can specify its own `model`, so routing high-volume, low-complexity work to Haiku controls cost while the coordinator keeps its model. Capping the coordinator's max_tokens breaks its output, not the subagent's cost. Removing tools disables the subagent. CLAUDE.md holds project memory, not per-subagent model selection.
referenceText: Subagents can route tasks to faster, cheaper models like Haiku to control costs.

stem: The team runs the same multi-step research procedure daily and keeps pasting identical instructions into Claude Code. What is the best way to make it a reusable, invokable command?
options: Create a custom slash command as a Markdown file with frontmatter, using `$ARGUMENTS` for the topic
Paste the procedure into a code comment in the repository
Store the procedure as a Git commit-message template
Increase the coordinator's `max_tokens` so it can hold more steps
rationale: Custom slash commands package a repeatable prompt/workflow into an invokable command, with frontmatter and argument placeholders. Code comments and commit templates are not executed by Claude Code as commands. max_tokens governs output length, not reusability.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: define reusable commands in Markdown with arguments.

stem: Every subagent (search, analysis, synthesis, report) must cite sources using the same numbered-footnote house style. Where should this shared convention live so all agents in the project pick it up automatically?
options: In CLAUDE.md at the project root
Duplicated into each developer's shell profile
In a top-level .gitignore entry
Only in the final report template, applied after generation completes
rationale: CLAUDE.md is loaded as project memory and shapes behavior across the session, making it the right home for shared conventions. Shell profiles and .gitignore are not read as instructions by Claude. Applying style only post-generation does not guide the agents that produce the citations.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md / memory: shared project instructions loaded automatically.

stem: You want a nightly CI job to run the research system unattended and emit machine-readable output for a downstream pipeline step. Which approach fits?
options: Run Claude Code in headless mode with `-p` and `--output-format json`
Launch the interactive TUI and leave it running overnight
Use plan mode so it pauses for human approval at each step
Disable all tools so the job cannot call external services
rationale: Headless mode (`-p`) is designed for non-interactive/CI use, and `--output-format json` yields structured output for downstream steps. The interactive TUI and plan mode both require a human in the loop. Disabling tools would prevent the research work entirely.
referenceText: Headless mode: run Claude Code non-interactively with -p and JSON output for CI.

stem: The web-search subagent needs to call a real search API exposed as a tool. In the Agent SDK, what is the idiomatic way to provide that capability?
options: Configure an MCP server in `mcpServers` so its tools become available to the agent
Paste representative search results into the system prompt before each run
Store the API key in CLAUDE.md so the model can reach the network directly
Fine-tune the model on a corpus of prior search results
rationale: MCP servers configured via `mcpServers` expose tools that the agent can invoke, which is the intended integration path in the Agent SDK. Pasting static results is not live search. A model cannot make network calls just because a key is in CLAUDE.md. Fine-tuning does not grant live tool access.
referenceText: Agent SDK MCP: configure mcpServers to expose external tools to the agent.

stem: The report-generation subagent must emit an object with `title`, `sections[]`, and `citations[]` that a downstream service parses programmatically. How do you make the output reliably schema-valid?
options: Use structured outputs (json_schema output format) with `required` fields and `additionalProperties: false`
Ask in the prompt for "valid JSON" and clean it up with a regex fallback
Raise the temperature so the model explores more JSON shapes
Return Markdown and let the downstream service infer the fields
rationale: Structured outputs constrain generation to a JSON schema, guaranteeing the required fields and rejecting stray keys with additionalProperties:false. Prompt-and-regex is brittle and not guaranteed. Higher temperature increases variability, the opposite of what is needed. Markdown requires fragile downstream parsing.
referenceText: Structured outputs: json_schema output format enforces required fields and no extra keys.

stem: When the web-search subagent emits a tool call, how should the application correctly continue the turn?
options: Execute the tool and send the output back as a `tool_result` content block referencing the `tool_use` id
Start a fresh conversation with no prior messages so the context stays small
Return the tool output as a new system prompt
Ignore the tool call and instruct the model to answer from its own knowledge
rationale: The tool-use loop requires returning a tool_result that references the originating tool_use id so the model can consume the output and continue. Restarting the conversation loses state. Tool output is not a system prompt. Ignoring the call defeats the purpose of tool use and invites hallucination.
referenceText: Tool use: return results as tool_result blocks referencing the tool_use id.

stem: The synthesis subagent formats citations inconsistently across reports. Which prompt technique most reliably standardizes the format?
options: Provide a few worked examples (few-shot) showing sources in and the exact citation format out
Append the instruction "be consistent" to the end of the prompt
Increase `max_tokens` so the model has room to format correctly
Remove all examples so the model is not confused by conflicting styles
rationale: Few-shot examples demonstrate the exact target format and are the most reliable way to lock in output style. A vague 'be consistent' instruction gives no concrete target. max_tokens governs length, not format. Removing examples removes the strongest available signal.
referenceText: Multishot prompting: examples reliably steer output format.

stem: The document-analysis subagent sometimes obeys text inside a source PDF as if it were an instruction. What is the best in-prompt mitigation?
options: Wrap each source in clearly named XML tags (e.g., <source>…</source>) to separate data from instructions
Concatenate all sources into a single unlabeled block of text
Interleave source text and task instructions line by line
Strip all delimiters so the model reads everything as continuous prose
rationale: XML tags create explicit structure that lets the model distinguish untrusted source data from your instructions. Unlabeled or interleaved text makes injection more likely, and removing delimiters removes the boundary entirely.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure prompts and separate data from instructions.

stem: A single mega-prompt telling one agent to "search, analyze, synthesize, and write the report" yields shallow, error-prone results. Which principle best improves reliability?
options: Decompose the work into discrete chained stages, passing each stage's output to the next
Raise the temperature to encourage more creative reasoning
Request all four outputs in one JSON blob to minimize token usage
Discard intermediate outputs to keep the context as small as possible
rationale: Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into focused subtasks, each easier to get right, and matches the coordinator/subagent design. Higher temperature adds variance, not rigor. Cramming everything into one blob reproduces the original problem. Discarding intermediates removes the grounding later stages depend on.
referenceText: Chain prompts: break complex tasks into sequential, focused steps.

stem: To reduce fabricated citations in the final report, which practices help? (Select 2.)
options: Instruct the model to cite only from the provided sources and to quote the supporting sentence
Require each claim to reference a source id present in the input, and drop claims that cannot be grounded
Increase the temperature so the model produces more citations
Allow the model to invent plausible DOIs when a source lacks one
Remove all source ids so individual citations cannot be traced
rationale: Constraining citations to supplied sources and requiring a traceable source id (dropping ungrounded claims) both directly curb fabrication. Raising temperature increases invention. Inventing DOIs is fabrication by design. Removing source ids destroys traceability and makes grounding impossible.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: ground responses in provided sources and require quoted support.

stem: A downstream parser breaks whenever the report-generation tool call includes unexpected extra fields. Which schema setting prevents this?
options: Set `additionalProperties: false` and enable strict tool use so only defined fields are allowed
Set `additionalProperties: true` to accept any shape the model produces
Omit `input_schema` so the model is unconstrained
Mark every property optional so nothing is enforced
rationale: additionalProperties:false combined with strict tool use guarantees the tool input contains only declared fields. Setting it true, omitting the schema, or making everything optional all loosen validation and permit the extra fields that break the parser.
referenceText: Structured outputs / strict tool use: additionalProperties:false forbids extra fields.

stem: The search MCP tool intermittently returns an error for certain queries. What is the right way to surface this so the agent can adapt?
options: Return the failure as a `tool_result` with `is_error: true` so the model can retry or reformulate the query
Throw an exception that aborts the entire coordinator run
Return an empty string as if the search had succeeded
Substitute fabricated results so the pipeline keeps moving
rationale: Returning a tool_result with is_error:true keeps the model in the loop so it can retry or adjust. Aborting the whole run is disproportionate for an intermittent failure. Faking success (empty or fabricated data) corrupts the report and hides the failure.
referenceText: Tool use error handling: return tool_result with is_error so the model can recover.

stem: A subagent completes its analysis but is low-confidence about a key figure. How should it hand results back so the coordinator can decide the next step?
options: Return a structured status including a confidence signal and any unresolved questions, not just prose
Return only the word "done" and let the coordinator assume success
Silently choose the most likely value and omit the uncertainty
Escalate by clearing the coordinator's context and restarting
rationale: Structured state (confidence, unresolved_questions) lets the coordinator route to verification or human review. A bare 'done' hides the uncertainty, silently guessing propagates possible errors, and wiping the coordinator's context destroys the run.
referenceText: Reliability: return structured status/state so the caller can handle uncertainty.

stem: The document-analysis subagent read roughly 200 pages across sources. What should it return to the synthesis subagent to keep the system reliable and within context limits?
options: A compact, structured summary of findings with source ids, not the raw pages
The full raw text of all 200 pages so nothing is lost
Only the total page count as a status metric
A file path the synthesis subagent has no permission to open
rationale: Passing a distilled, source-tagged summary keeps the downstream agent within context and preserves traceability. Forwarding all raw text defeats the purpose of the subagent and can overflow context. A page count carries no findings, and an unreadable path is unusable.
referenceText: Subagents return distilled summaries to preserve downstream context.

stem: Which statement best explains how delegating to subagents helps the coordinator stay within its context window on a long research run?
options: Exploration happens in the subagent's separate context; only the distilled result returns to the coordinator
Subagents remove the token limit from the coordinator's context window
Subagents compress the underlying model weights at runtime
Subagents operate with no context window of their own
