stem: Extraction accuracy drops on invoices where the correct line-item total requires reconciling subtotals, discounts, and tax. Forcing a pure-JSON tool call gives Claude no room to reason and errors increase. What is the best way to preserve reasoning while still getting clean structured output?
options: Add a free-text 'reasoning' string as a required field inside the extraction schema and parse around it downstream
Remove all complex computed fields so no reasoning is ever needed
Instruct Claude to compute silently and never show any intermediate steps
Let Claude reason first (via extended thinking or a scratchpad step) and then emit the final answer through the extraction tool, keeping reasoning out of the schema fields
rationale: Chain-of-thought/extended thinking improves accuracy on multi-step reconciliation; the pattern is to reason before producing the final structured payload so the JSON stays clean and the reasoning does not pollute the schema. Stuffing free-form reasoning into a schema field couples brittle prose to your data contract. Removing needed fields sacrifices requirements. Forbidding reasoning entirely reintroduces the accuracy problem.
referenceText: Chain-of-thought / extended thinking before structured output

stem: Business stakeholders require that low-certainty extractions never post automatically to the ERP. How should you design the pipeline so uncertain results are handled gracefully rather than silently accepted?
options: Post everything to the ERP and rely on accountants to notice mistakes later
Retry uncertain documents at temperature 0 until Claude produces any answer, then post it
Include a per-record confidence signal in the tool output and route records below a threshold to a human-review queue instead of auto-posting
Reject and delete any document Claude is unsure about
rationale: Emitting a structured confidence signal and escalating sub-threshold records to human review is the canonical graceful-degradation/escalation pattern for high-stakes downstream integration. Auto-posting everything pushes cost of errors downstream. Retrying at temperature 0 does not raise genuine confidence, it just reduces variance. Deleting uncertain documents loses legitimate records that a human could resolve.
referenceText: Increase consistency — thresholds and human escalation

stem: Each request concatenates a long raw OCR dump of the invoice directly after your instructions with no delimiters, and Claude sometimes confuses instruction text with document content. What is the recommended prompt structure to prevent this?
options: Put the document in ALL CAPS so Claude knows it is data
Wrap the document body in clearly named XML tags (e.g., <document>...</document>) so Claude can unambiguously separate instructions from content
Move the instructions to after the document and remove any labeling
Base64-encode the document so it cannot be mistaken for instructions
rationale: XML tags are the documented way to delimit and label distinct parts of a prompt, letting Claude reliably tell instructions from the document to be processed. Capitalization is not a structural boundary. Reordering without labels does not resolve the ambiguity. Base64 obscures the text Claude needs to read and wastes tokens.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure prompts

stem: Some contracts run to hundreds of pages and exceed a comfortable single-call context budget, yet you must extract a structured summary of every clause. Which approach best balances completeness and reliability?
options: Truncate the document to the first N pages that fit and extract only from those
Send the whole document at once and accept whatever fits, ignoring overflow
Summarize the document into a paragraph first, then extract fields from the paragraph
Split the document into sections, extract structured data per section, then merge and de-duplicate the partial results into the final schema
rationale: Chunking by section, extracting per chunk, and merging is the standard way to keep extraction complete and within context limits while preserving fidelity. Truncation and 'whatever fits' both silently drop clauses. Pre-summarizing to a paragraph destroys the granular clause-level detail the schema requires before extraction even begins.
referenceText: Context windows — managing long inputs

stem: You want to maximize the rate at which Claude returns output that conforms to your JSON schema. Which two techniques most directly and reliably improve schema conformance? (Select 2.)
options: Define the target schema as a tool input_schema and force the tool call
Raise temperature to 1.0 to increase output diversity
Provide a few-shot example showing a correctly-formatted output object
Ask Claude to 'try your best' to match the schema
Delete all property descriptions to keep the prompt short
rationale: Forcing a tool call with the schema as input_schema and supplying a concrete few-shot example are both documented, high-leverage ways to increase conformance. High temperature increases variance and hurts conformance. A vague 'try your best' gives no structural constraint. Removing property descriptions strips the guidance that helps Claude place values correctly.
referenceText: Increase output consistency (JSON) and multishot prompting

stem: Your source invoices arrive as PDFs containing both text and tables. A teammate proposes running a separate OCR tool first and pasting the raw text into the prompt, which loses table structure. What does Claude support that better preserves layout for extraction?
options: Convert the PDF to a single JPEG and describe it in prose
Pass the PDF directly in a document content block so Claude can read text and visual layout together
Strip the PDF to plain text before sending, since Claude cannot read PDFs
Email the PDF to a human to transcribe into JSON manually
rationale: Claude's PDF support lets you pass the PDF as a document block and reason over both its text and visual/tabular layout, which is more faithful than lossy pre-OCR. Flattening to one JPEG loses multi-page fidelity and text quality. It is false that Claude cannot read PDFs. Manual transcription defeats the automated pipeline.
referenceText: PDF support

stem: A 'payment_status' field must be exactly one of: paid, unpaid, partial, void. Claude occasionally returns near-synonyms like 'settled' or 'not paid,' breaking downstream joins. What schema construct best constrains this field?
options: Add a prose sentence asking Claude to 'please use standard statuses'
Make payment_status a free-text string and normalize synonyms with a lookup table afterward only
Set payment_status to type integer and map statuses to numbers in the prompt
Define payment_status as a string with an enum of the four allowed values in the tool input_schema
rationale: An enum in the input_schema constrains the field to the exact permitted values, which is the direct fix for categorical drift. A prose request is not enforceable. Relying solely on post-hoc synonym mapping is brittle and always trails new variants. Encoding statuses as opaque integers loses semantics and invites off-by-one mapping errors.
referenceText: Tool use — JSON schema and input_schema constraints

stem: Re-running extraction on the same invoice sometimes yields slightly different numeric values, which complicates reconciliation and auditing downstream. Which single change most improves run-to-run stability for a deterministic extraction task?
options: Set temperature to 1 to average over more samples
Randomize the field order in the schema on each call
Set temperature to 0 so sampling is greedy and outputs are as consistent as possible for identical inputs
Increase top_p to 1.0 and leave temperature high
rationale: For deterministic extraction where you want the most consistent output for a fixed input, temperature 0 (greedy decoding) is the right setting. Higher temperature and high top_p both increase variability. Randomizing field order introduces avoidable nondeterminism in the contract itself.
referenceText: Increase output consistency — temperature control

stem: Reviewers find Claude misclassifies which monetary value is the 'grand_total' versus the 'subtotal.' The schema currently lists both as bare number fields with no explanation. What is the most effective, low-cost fix?
options: Rename the fields to field_a and field_b to avoid biasing Claude
Write precise natural-language descriptions on each property in the input_schema clarifying exactly what grand_total and subtotal mean and where to find them
Remove one of the two fields to eliminate the confusion
Add ten more numeric fields so Claude has more context
rationale: Property-level descriptions in the schema are read by the model and are the primary lever for disambiguating semantically similar fields. Opaque names remove the very cues that guide correct placement. Deleting a required field loses needed data. Adding unrelated fields increases confusion rather than resolving it.
referenceText: Tool use — write detailed tool/field descriptions

stem: During a bulk backfill your extraction service starts receiving HTTP 429 and 529 responses and some records fail. What is the correct reliability handling for these specific errors?
options: Immediately retry in a tight loop with no delay until each request succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent failures and drop those records
Lower the model's temperature so it stops returning 429 errors
Retry the failed requests with exponential backoff and jitter, respecting rate limits, rather than hammering the API immediately
rationale: 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) are transient; exponential backoff with jitter is the documented way to recover without amplifying load. Tight-loop retries worsen the overload and can extend throttling. They are not permanent failures, so dropping records loses data. Temperature is a sampling parameter and has nothing to do with HTTP status codes.
referenceText: API errors and rate limits — handling 429/529

stem: You must extract structured data from 500,000 archived invoices overnight. Latency per document does not matter, but cost and throughput do, and results are consumed the next morning. Which Anthropic capability best fits this workload?
options: Send 500,000 synchronous requests as fast as possible from one thread
Open a single streaming request and stuff all documents into one prompt
Submit the documents through the Message Batches API for high-volume asynchronous processing at reduced cost
Ask a human team to process them in real time to guarantee accuracy
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large-volume, latency-tolerant asynchronous jobs and lowers cost—ideal for an overnight backfill. Firehosing synchronous requests hits rate limits and is fragile. One giant prompt cannot hold 500k documents and destroys per-document isolation. Manual processing does not scale to this volume.
referenceText: Message Batches API (batch processing)

stem: Every extraction request repeats the same long system prompt: detailed field descriptions, the JSON schema, and a dozen few-shot examples. Across thousands of documents this is costly and slow. Which feature reduces cost and latency for the unchanging prefix while keeping accuracy?
options: Delete the few-shot examples to shorten every prompt
Use prompt caching on the stable system prompt, schema, and examples so the shared prefix is reused across calls
Move the schema into the user message so it changes each call
Compress the schema by removing whitespace only
rationale: Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix (instructions, schema, examples) across many requests, cutting cost and latency without sacrificing the guidance that drives accuracy. Deleting examples reduces accuracy. Moving the schema into a per-call, varying position defeats caching. Whitespace stripping yields negligible savings and does not address repeated processing of the prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching

stem: Your extraction agent must fetch source documents from an internal document store and expose them to Claude in a standardized, tool-agnostic way, so the same integration works across multiple clients. Which Model Context Protocol construct is the right fit for exposing read-only document content?
options: Hard-code each document's bytes inline in the system prompt of every request
Store documents only in the model's training data
Rename your REST endpoints and call them MCP without implementing the protocol
Expose the documents as MCP resources served by an MCP server the client connects to
rationale: MCP resources are the standardized way for an MCP server to expose read-only content (like documents) that clients and models can consume, giving the tool-agnostic integration described. Inlining bytes per request is not standardized and does not scale. Models are not document stores. Merely relabeling REST endpoints does not make them MCP-compliant.
referenceText: MCP concepts — Resources

stem: Your invoice extractor occasionally receives a document that is not an invoice at all (e.g., a cover letter). You want it to fail gracefully instead of fabricating invoice fields. Which two design choices best achieve this? (Select 2.)
options: Lower temperature so Claude extracts invoice fields more confidently from the non-invoice
Add a document_type / is_expected_document flag to the output so Claude can signal a mismatch instead of forcing extraction
Always populate every field with best-guess values to satisfy the required schema
Instruct Claude to return nulls with an explicit status when the document is not an invoice, rather than inventing values
Remove the schema entirely so any output is accepted
rationale: A document-type flag plus an instruction to emit nulls and a status when the input is off-type lets the system detect and route mismatches gracefully. Lowering temperature only makes fabricated extractions more confident. Forcing best-guess values into required fields produces convincing garbage. Dropping the schema abandons the very contract downstream systems depend on.
referenceText: Reduce hallucinations — allow abstention and flag mismatches

stem: You want a regression gate that runs your extraction over a fixed set of sample documents and asserts the outputs still validate against the JSON schema on every pull request, with no interactive terminal. Which approach fits a CI environment?
options: Require an engineer to open an interactive Claude Code session and eyeball the outputs before each merge
Skip validation in CI and only check schemas manually before releases
Run the extraction non-interactively using the Claude Agent SDK / Claude Code headless mode in the CI job and fail the build if schema validation fails
Commit the expected JSON and never re-run extraction so CI stays green
rationale: Running extraction non-interactively via the Agent SDK / Claude Code headless mode lets CI execute the pipeline and assert schema validity automatically on every PR, which is the point of a regression gate. Manual eyeballing does not scale and is not automated. Skipping CI validation defers failures to production. Freezing expected output without re-running means the gate never actually tests the extractor.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK — headless / programmatic use

stem: Your pipeline must return a payload that always conforms to a fixed customer-record shape (name, email, account_id) so it can be inserted directly into a database. Which approach most reliably guarantees Claude emits that structure on every call?
options: Define a single tool whose input_schema is the record shape and set tool_choice to {"type":"tool","name":...} so Claude must return a tool_use block matching it
Ask Claude in the system prompt to 'respond only with JSON' and parse the assistant's free text
Add a stop sequence of '}' so generation always halts at the end of the object
Lower temperature to 0 and instruct Claude to avoid any prose
rationale: Forcing a single extraction tool via tool_choice makes Claude return a tool_use block whose input is validated against your input_schema, the canonical way to get reliable structured JSON. Prompting for JSON in prose (1) still allows preambles, markdown fences, and drift. A '}' stop sequence (2) truncates nested objects and breaks on the first inner brace. Temperature 0 (3) reduces variance but does not constrain structure.
referenceText: To have Claude call a function that you define, pass a tool with an input_schema; set tool_choice to require a specific tool call rather than rely on prompting.

stem: Extraction usually returns valid JSON, but about 2% of calls include an extra field or an enum value that isn't in your schema, breaking the downstream insert. You already force a single extraction tool. What is the most direct fix?
options: Add strict:true to the tool definition so tool calls are constrained to exactly match the schema
Raise temperature so Claude explores more schema-consistent completions
Increase max_tokens so Claude has more room to finish the object
Remove the enum from the schema and validate the values only in downstream code
rationale: Strict tool use (strict:true) constrains generation so tool names and inputs conform exactly to the declared schema, eliminating stray fields and out-of-enum values. Higher temperature (1) increases variance, making drift worse. More max_tokens (2) addresses truncation, not conformance. Dropping the enum (3) hides the problem instead of enforcing valid values.
referenceText: Add strict:true to your custom tool definitions to guarantee schema validation on tool names and inputs.

stem: Your extraction schema declares invoice_total as {"type":"number","minimum":0} and uses strict structured outputs. A stakeholder assumes negative totals are now structurally impossible. What must you tell them?
options: Numeric range keywords like minimum are not enforced by structured outputs, so you must still validate the value in your application
minimum is enforced, so a negative total can never be returned
Structured outputs enforce minimum only when temperature is set to 0
Adding strict:true will begin enforcing minimum automatically
rationale: Structured outputs support types, enum, required, and additionalProperties but do not enforce numeric constraints such as minimum/maximum/multipleOf or string length constraints; those must be validated downstream. Options 1, 2, and 3 all assert enforcement that the feature explicitly does not provide.
referenceText: Not supported: numerical constraints (minimum, maximum, multipleOf) and string constraints (minLength, maxLength).

stem: Some source documents simply do not contain a purchase-order number. Downstream code must distinguish 'the document has no PO number' from a wrong guess. How should you design the schema and prompt?
options: Make po_number nullable and instruct Claude to return null when the field is absent from the document
Instruct Claude to infer the most likely PO number from surrounding context
Omit the field entirely whenever it is missing so the object stays small
Reject any document that lacks a PO number before sending it to Claude
rationale: A nullable field plus an explicit instruction to emit null when information is absent lets downstream code reliably tell 'not present' from a value, which is the core of graceful edge-case handling. Inferring a value (1) invites hallucination. Silently omitting the field (2) makes 'missing' and 'not extracted' indistinguishable and can violate required. Rejecting the document (3) discards valid data for an expected case.
referenceText: Basic types include null; make a field nullable and instruct Claude to return null when the value is not present.

stem: Accuracy on noisy scanned contracts is below target. Which two techniques are most likely to improve extraction quality without changing the schema? (Select 2.)
options: Provide several few-shot examples that pair tricky input snippets with the exact correct JSON
Let Claude reason in a scratchpad or thinking step before emitting the final structured output
Raise temperature to 1.0 to increase output diversity
Remove the schema so Claude is less constrained
Rely on the model's parametric knowledge instead of showing any examples
rationale: Diverse few-shot examples of the hard cases and giving Claude room to reason before it answers are both well-established accuracy levers for extraction. Raising temperature (2) increases variance and error on a deterministic task. Removing the schema (3) removes constraints that aid correctness. Leaning on parametric knowledge (4) is the opposite of grounding in the document.
referenceText: Include a few diverse, relevant examples of input mapped to correct output; letting Claude think step by step before answering improves accuracy on complex tasks.

stem: You decide to add examples to your extraction prompt. Which set of examples will help the most?
options: Three to five diverse examples that include the messy edge cases you actually see, each mapped to correct JSON
A single clean, ideal example so Claude isn't confused by variety
Ten near-identical examples of the easiest document type
Examples of the input documents only, without the expected JSON output
rationale: Multishot prompting works best when the handful of examples are diverse and cover the edge cases the model must handle, each paired with the correct target output. One example (1) underspecifies; ten near-identical easy examples (2) teach nothing about hard cases; input-only examples (3) fail to demonstrate the target mapping.
referenceText: Include 3-5 diverse, relevant examples that cover edge cases, each showing the desired output.

stem: For a lightweight path you call the Messages API without tools and want the assistant's first characters to be valid JSON, never a preamble like 'Here is the data:'. Which technique enforces that?
options: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening '{' so Claude continues the JSON object directly
Add 'do not write a preamble' to the user message and hope Claude complies
Set a stop sequence of 'Here is' to cut off any preamble
Send the request twice and keep whichever response happens to start with a brace
rationale: Prefilling the assistant message with '{' constrains Claude to continue from that token, guaranteeing the response opens the JSON object with no preamble. A prompt instruction (1) is not a guarantee. A 'Here is' stop sequence (2) is brittle and matches only one phrasing. Sampling twice (3) is wasteful and still unreliable.
referenceText: Prefill Claude's response with the beginning of the desired output (e.g. '{') to skip preambles and control formatting.

stem: Extractions that require multi-step reasoning (for example computing a total from line items) are more accurate when Claude thinks first, but you still need clean structured output. What is the best design?
options: Let Claude reason in an extended-thinking block or scratchpad, then emit the result through the extraction tool so the JSON stays clean
Put the chain of reasoning inside a free-text field of the same JSON object and strip it out later
Forbid all reasoning to keep latency low and accept the accuracy loss
Ask for the answer twice and diff the two JSON objects to pick a winner
rationale: Separating the reasoning (extended thinking or a scratchpad) from the final tool call preserves accuracy while keeping the structured payload clean. Embedding reasoning in a JSON field (1) pollutes the schema and risks malformed output. Forbidding reasoning (2) sacrifices the accuracy you need. Double-sampling and diffing (3) adds cost without addressing the reasoning need.
referenceText: Extended thinking lets Claude work through problems step by step before producing a final answer.

stem: Your app validates each tool_use payload against the JSON schema. When validation fails, what is the most effective way to let Claude fix it within the same conversation?
options: Return a tool_result marked is_error:true that describes the specific validation failures so Claude can correct the fields and try again
Silently drop the response and re-send the original request from scratch
Lower temperature and resend without telling Claude what was wrong
Raise the error to the end user and stop the pipeline
rationale: Returning an is_error tool_result that names the exact validation problems gives Claude the feedback it needs to self-correct in the next turn, the intended error-handling loop for tool use. Re-sending blindly (1) discards useful context and repeats the mistake. Resending silently (2) gives Claude no signal. Escalating immediately (3) forgoes an easy automated recovery.
referenceText: If a tool call is invalid, return a tool_result with is_error set to true and a description so Claude can retry.

stem: Extracting every line item from long invoices, you sometimes get JSON that ends abruptly and won't parse. The response's stop_reason is 'max_tokens'. What should your code do?
options: Detect stop_reason == 'max_tokens' and raise max_tokens (or continue the response) before treating the JSON as complete
Assume the object is complete and just repair the trailing brace
Lower temperature so the output becomes shorter
Treat it as a transient error and retry the identical request unchanged
rationale: A stop_reason of max_tokens means the output was truncated; the fix is to detect that reason and give the model room to finish (or continue generation) before parsing. Repairing the brace (1) fabricates data for the missing items. Lowering temperature (2) does not shorten a genuinely long payload. Retrying unchanged (3) will truncate again at the same limit.
referenceText: A stop_reason of max_tokens indicates the response was cut off by the token limit; increase max_tokens or continue the generation.

stem: The business requires high precision: uncertain extractions must go to a human rather than flow straight to the ledger. Which design best supports this?
options: Add a confidence field to the schema, set a threshold, and route records below it to human review while auto-accepting the rest
Send every extraction to human review to be completely safe
Auto-accept everything and let downstream systems catch the errors
Discard any record Claude appears unsure about
rationale: A confidence signal plus a threshold gives you an escalation policy: low-confidence records go to a human, high-confidence ones flow through, balancing precision and throughput. Reviewing everything (1) defeats automation. Auto-accepting all (2) violates the precision requirement. Discarding uncertain records (3) loses recoverable data.
referenceText: Design agents to escalate to human review when confidence is low rather than acting on uncertain output.

stem: A nightly Agent SDK job extracts data from thousands of documents and occasionally crashes midway. Reruns must not reprocess completed documents or lose progress. What is the right approach?
options: Persist per-document status (pending/done/failed) in external structured state so a rerun resumes where it left off
Keep the list of processed documents in the conversation prompt and rely on the model to remember them
Restart from the first document every run to guarantee completeness
Store progress only in memory so it is fast, accepting loss on a crash
rationale: Durable, external structured state that tracks each document's status makes the job resumable and idempotent across crashes. Relying on the prompt/model memory (1) is not durable and does not scale to thousands of items. Restarting from scratch (2) wastes work and risks duplicate writes. In-memory-only state (3) is lost on the very crashes you must survive.
referenceText: Persist progress and state externally so long-running agent jobs are resumable and idempotent rather than depending on in-context memory.

stem: One source document is far larger than the context window, yet you still need a complete extraction. What is the most reliable strategy?
options: Split the document into coherent sections, extract from each, then merge and de-duplicate the results
Truncate the document to fit the window and extract from the first part only
Increase temperature so Claude compresses the document itself
Send the whole document and ignore the resulting context-length error
rationale: Chunking into coherent sections, extracting per chunk, then merging and de-duplicating yields a complete result within context limits. Truncating (1) drops data. Temperature (2) has nothing to do with fitting content in context. Ignoring the error (3) means no extraction at all.
referenceText: When input exceeds the context window, split it into sections, process each independently, and combine the results.

stem: Under load your extraction service receives HTTP 429 (rate limited) and 529 (overloaded) responses. What is the correct client behavior?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring any Retry-After hint, up to a bounded number of attempts
Retry immediately in a tight loop until it eventually succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent failures and drop the document
Switch to a larger model on every 429 you receive
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; the correct response is bounded exponential backoff with jitter, respecting Retry-After. Tight-loop retries (1) worsen congestion. Treating them as permanent (2) discards recoverable work. Switching models (3) neither fixes rate limiting nor addresses overload and can raise cost.
referenceText: 429 rate_limit and 529 overloaded errors are transient; retry with exponential backoff.

stem: You want CI to fail whenever a prompt change causes Claude to emit output that violates the extraction schema. Which setup fits best?
options: Run Claude Code headlessly (claude -p) over fixture documents in CI and fail the job if any output fails schema validation
Manually spot-check a handful of outputs before each release
Disable schema validation in CI because it slows the build down
Only validate in production so CI stays fast
rationale: Headless Claude Code (claude -p) runs non-interactively in CI, so you can extract against fixtures and fail the build on schema violations, catching regressions before release. Manual spot checks (1) are not reproducible gates. Disabling validation (2) and validating only in production (3) let regressions ship.
referenceText: Use headless mode (claude -p) to run Claude Code non-interactively in CI and scripts.

stem: You expose the extractor as an MCP server tool so several internal clients can reuse it. Which statement about the tool's interface is correct?
options: Each MCP tool declares a JSON Schema inputSchema that describes and constrains its arguments for any connecting client
MCP tools cannot declare schemas, so clients must guess the arguments
MCP tool arguments are always free-form strings with no schema
An MCP tool can only be called by the client that originally defined it
rationale: MCP tools advertise a name, description, and a JSON Schema inputSchema, so any connecting client/host knows and can validate the expected arguments, which is what makes the extractor reusable. Options 1 and 2 contradict the tool definition. Option 3 misstates MCP: tools are exposed by a server for any authorized client to call.
referenceText: Each tool is defined with a name, description, and an inputSchema (JSON Schema) describing its parameters.

stem: Your schema's status enum is ["Open","Closed"], but the database expects lowercase and Claude occasionally returns 'open'. Structured outputs are enabled. What is the robust fix?
options: Normalize (case-fold) enum values before insert, because exact enum capitalization is not guaranteed
Assume casing always matches the schema and insert the values verbatim
Remove the enum so any string is accepted downstream
Raise max_tokens so Claude has room to match the exact case
rationale: Structured outputs constrain values to the enum set but do not guarantee exact capitalization, so normalizing before insert is the robust integration step. Trusting exact casing (1) will intermittently break the insert. Removing the enum (2) sacrifices validation. max_tokens (3) is unrelated to casing.
referenceText: Structured outputs don't guarantee the capitalization of string enum and const values; compare enum values case-insensitively.

stem: Which two design choices make your extractor degrade gracefully on unexpected or malformed documents rather than fail hard? (Select 2.)
options: Use nullable fields and instruct Claude to return null for information the document doesn't contain
Return validation errors to Claude via an is_error tool_result so it can self-correct
Configure the schema to force a best-guess value for every field, even when the data is absent
Abort the entire batch the first time a single document fails to parse
Disable schema validation so that nothing can ever be rejected
rationale: Nullable fields with an explicit null-when-absent instruction, plus an is_error feedback loop that lets Claude fix invalid output, together let the system handle edge cases without crashing. Forcing best-guess values (2) manufactures wrong data. Aborting the whole batch on one failure (3) is the opposite of graceful degradation. Disabling validation (4) removes the safety net entirely.
referenceText: Return a tool_result with is_error true so Claude can correct invalid output; use nullable fields for information that may be absent.

stem: Claude's final turn should return structured data to the caller as its response (it is not invoking one of your functions). Which structured-output mechanism fits best?
options: Use the JSON output format (output_config.format) to constrain Claude's response to your schema
Define a dummy tool and force it even though no function will actually execute
Parse the schema out of a free-text response with a regular expression
Prefill the entire JSON yourself and ignore whatever Claude produces
rationale: When you want Claude's own response (not a function call) to match a schema, the JSON output format (output_config.format) is the purpose-built feature; strict tool use is for validating calls to functions you execute. A dummy forced tool (1) is a workaround that muddles intent. Regex parsing (2) is brittle. Prefilling everything (3) discards the extraction you asked for.
referenceText: JSON outputs control Claude's response format; strict tool use validates parameters of tool calls. They solve different problems.

stem: Several engineers run the extraction task from Claude Code and get inconsistent field naming because each writes their own prompt. What is the best way to standardize it?
options: Codify the schema and extraction instructions in a project slash command (and CLAUDE.md) so every run uses the same definition
Ask each engineer to memorize the schema and paste it manually each time
Raise temperature so the different prompts converge naturally
Rely on the model to infer a consistent schema across different users
rationale: A shared project slash command backed by CLAUDE.md gives everyone one authoritative, version-controlled prompt and schema, eliminating per-engineer drift. Manual pasting (1) reintroduces inconsistency. Temperature (2) cannot reconcile divergent prompts. Hoping the model infers consistency (3) is exactly the failure being observed.
referenceText: Define reusable prompts as project slash commands and shared instructions in CLAUDE.md so the team runs them consistently.

stem: Your extraction service must return only a JSON object that conforms to a fixed schema (invoice number, vendor, line items, total) so a downstream ERP can ingest it without a parsing layer. Which approach most reliably guarantees the model emits data matching that structure?
options: Define a tool whose input_schema is the target JSON schema and set tool_choice to force that specific tool, then read the structured tool_use input
Add a system prompt line saying "Respond with valid JSON only and never include prose," then json.loads the text block
Set temperature to 0 so the model deterministically produces the same JSON shape every time
Append a stop_sequence of "}" so generation halts as soon as the JSON object closes
rationale: Forcing a specific tool via tool_choice makes the model return its output as a structured tool_use block whose input is validated against the tool's input_schema (JSON Schema), which is the canonical way to guarantee schema-shaped output. A prose instruction to 'only output JSON' is not enforced and frequently leaks explanatory text or markdown fences. Temperature 0 reduces variability but does not constrain structure. A stop_sequence of '}' would truncate any object that legitimately contains nested braces, corrupting output.
referenceText: By forcing Claude to use a tool, you can guarantee that its response conforms to the tool's input_schema.

stem: You are extracting without tool use and want the very first token of the response to begin the JSON object so no leading prose appears. Which technique directly forces this?
options: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening "{" so the model continues the JSON from that point
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to finish the object before commentary
Set top_p to 0.1 to suppress the model's tendency to add explanations
Add "IMPORTANT" in all caps before the instruction to output JSON
rationale: Prefilling the assistant message with '{' constrains the model to continue from that character, eliminating preamble and forcing JSON to start immediately. Raising max_tokens only affects length, not whether prose precedes the object. Lowering top_p changes sampling diversity but does not remove leading text. Emphasis words nudge but do not guarantee the first token.
referenceText: You can pre-fill part of Claude's response in the assistant turn to guide it to skip preambles and output in a specific format.

stem: A downstream JSON Schema validator rejects roughly 3% of extractions (wrong types, missing required keys). You want a resilient pipeline that recovers automatically without a human. What is the most effective reliability pattern?
options: Catch the validation error, send the specific validator message back to the model, and ask it to correct the extraction, retrying up to a bounded number of times
Discard any record that fails validation and log it so the batch can complete on schedule
Loosen the schema to make all fields optional so validation always passes
Lower the temperature to 0 and resubmit the identical request until it validates
rationale: Feeding the concrete validation error back to the model gives it the exact defect to fix, and a bounded retry loop prevents infinite cost while recovering most transient failures. Discarding records loses data the task requires. Loosening the schema defeats the purpose of validation and lets bad data reach the ERP. Resubmitting the identical prompt at temperature 0 will deterministically reproduce the same failure.
referenceText: When a tool result or downstream check fails, return the error to Claude so it can adjust and retry rather than failing the whole run.

stem: Some source documents omit the customer's tax ID. Your schema currently marks tax_id as a required string, and the model sometimes invents a plausible-looking ID when it is absent. What is the best schema and prompting fix?
options: Make tax_id nullable (type ["string", "null"]) and instruct the model to return null when the value is not present in the document
Keep tax_id required and add a few-shot example showing a fabricated ID so the model matches the pattern
Remove tax_id from the schema entirely so the model cannot fill it incorrectly
Raise max_tokens so the model has room to reason its way to the correct ID
rationale: A nullable field plus an explicit instruction to emit null for absent data gives the model a correct, non-hallucinated way to represent missing information while keeping the field in the contract. Adding a fabricated example teaches hallucination. Removing the field loses a value that is present in most documents. More tokens do not help when the datum simply is not in the source.
referenceText: Give Claude a way to say it doesn't know or that a value is absent, such as a null or 'not found' option, to reduce hallucination.

stem: Accuracy is poor on documents with unusual layouts and ambiguous phrasing. Without changing the model or schema, which two prompt-engineering techniques most directly improve extraction quality on these edge cases? (Select 2.)
options: Include few-shot examples that specifically cover the tricky layouts and their correct structured output
Let the model reason step by step about the document before it produces the final structured output
Increase temperature so the model explores more interpretations of ambiguous text
Remove field descriptions from the schema so the model is not biased by them
rationale: Few-shot examples that mirror the hard cases teach the desired mapping directly, and allowing chain-of-thought reasoning before the structured output improves correctness on ambiguous inputs. Raising temperature increases variance and makes extraction less reliable. Stripping field descriptions removes guidance the model uses to place values correctly.
referenceText: Providing examples (multishot prompting) and letting Claude think step by step both improve accuracy on complex tasks.

stem: A category field is an enum of ['hardware','software','services']. Occasionally a document describes something that fits none of these. Currently the model force-fits the closest value, corrupting analytics. What is the cleanest fix?
options: Add an explicit 'other' (or 'unknown') member to the enum and instruct the model to use it when no listed category applies
Convert category to a free-text string so the model can write whatever it observes
Instruct the model to always pick the statistically most common category when unsure
Remove the enum constraint and rely on the downstream system to normalize the value
rationale: An explicit 'other'/'unknown' enum member gives the model a valid escape hatch, preserving a closed vocabulary while preventing forced miscategorization. Free text destroys the controlled vocabulary analytics depend on. Defaulting to the most common category deliberately injects wrong data. Dropping the enum pushes the ambiguity downstream instead of resolving it at extraction.
referenceText: Enums with a catch-all value let the model represent out-of-vocabulary cases without inventing data.

stem: You must extract structured records from 50,000 archived contracts overnight. Latency per document does not matter, but per-document cost does, and the job can complete any time before morning. Which API approach best fits?
options: Submit the documents through the Message Batches API for asynchronous, lower-cost processing
Open 50,000 concurrent streaming requests to finish as fast as possible
Send the documents one at a time synchronously to keep the code simple
Combine all 50,000 documents into a single request to amortize overhead
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant workloads and offers reduced cost, matching an overnight bulk job exactly. Massive concurrent streaming maximizes speed and rate-limit pressure but not cost efficiency. Sequential synchronous calls are simple but slow and no cheaper. Packing everything into one request would blow past the context window and lose per-document isolation.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at reduced cost, ideal for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: Every extraction request includes the same lengthy system prompt, the full JSON schema, and a large block of few-shot examples; only the document text changes. You want to cut cost and latency on this repeated prefix. What should you use?
options: Enable prompt caching on the static prefix (instructions, schema, examples) so it is reused across requests
Move the schema and examples into a fine-tuned model so they need not be sent
Truncate the few-shot examples to a single example to shrink each request
Send the static content once and rely on the model to remember it across requests
rationale: Prompt caching stores the large, unchanging prefix so subsequent requests reuse it at lower cost and latency, which is exactly this pattern. Fine-tuning is not offered as a substitute here and is far heavier than needed. Cutting examples degrades accuracy to save cost. The API is stateless between requests, so the model cannot 'remember' prior content.
referenceText: Prompt caching lets you reuse large, static portions of your prompt across requests, reducing cost and latency.

stem: A subset of your documents are 400-page regulatory filings that exceed the model's context window even after trimming. You still need complete structured extraction from them. What is the soundest strategy?
options: Split each filing into coherent sections, extract structured data per section, then merge the partial results into one validated record
Truncate each filing to the first N pages that fit and extract only from those
Summarize the whole filing first, then extract from the short summary
Retry the full document repeatedly until a request happens to fit
rationale: Chunking into meaningful sections, extracting per chunk, and merging preserves full coverage while respecting the context limit. Truncation drops data that may contain required fields. Summarizing first is lossy and can discard the exact values you need to extract verbatim. Retrying an over-length request never makes it fit; it simply fails repeatedly.
referenceText: Requests that exceed the context window must be split; process long documents in chunks and combine the results.

stem: Your invoices arrive as scanned image PDFs (no embedded text layer). Your current pipeline runs a lossy OCR step to plain text before extraction, and numbers in cramped tables are frequently misread. How can you improve fidelity with the model itself?
options: Pass the PDF directly to the model using document content blocks so it reads the page layout and text natively
Increase the OCR engine's DPI and keep feeding the resulting text to the model
Ask the model to guess the most likely digits when OCR output looks garbled
Convert the PDF to a bulleted text summary before sending it to the model
rationale: The model's PDF support lets it read the document (including visual layout and tables) directly, bypassing a lossy OCR bottleneck that is corrupting the numbers. Tuning OCR DPI still routes everything through the same lossy pre-step. Instructing the model to guess digits invites fabrication. Summarizing to bullets discards the precise tabular values extraction needs.
referenceText: Claude can process PDF documents directly, understanding both text and visual layout without a separate OCR step.

stem: Auditors require that every extracted contract value can be traced to the exact sentence in the source document it came from. Which model capability provides this grounding directly?
options: Enable Citations so the model attaches references to the source passages that support each extracted value
Ask the model to append the page number it thinks each value came from as an extra field
Log the full model response and manually search the document for each value later
Lower temperature so the model copies source text more literally
rationale: The Citations feature returns verifiable references to the source spans that support the output, giving auditors traceability by design. Asking the model to self-report page numbers is unverified and can be hallucinated. Manual post-hoc search does not scale and is error-prone. Low temperature affects wording, not traceability.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages it uses to generate responses.

stem: Your extraction prompt concatenates lengthy instructions, the JSON schema, and the raw document into one string, and the model sometimes treats instructions inside the document as commands. What is the recommended way to delimit the source document?
options: Wrap the document in clearly named XML tags (for example <document>...</document>) so the model distinguishes content from instructions
Prefix the document with the word DOCUMENT in capitals and hope the model notices
Base64-encode the document so its text cannot be interpreted as instructions
Place the document before all instructions so the model reads it first
rationale: XML tags are the documented technique for structurally separating input content from instructions, which reduces instruction bleed-through. A capitalized label is far weaker and easily ignored. Base64 encoding makes the document unreadable to the model, defeating extraction. Reordering does not create a clear boundary between data and instructions.
referenceText: Use XML tags to clearly separate different parts of your prompt, such as instructions, examples, and input data.

stem: Business rules say extractions the model is unsure about must go to a human reviewer, while confident ones flow straight through. How should you design the extraction contract to support this routing?
options: Add a numeric confidence field to the schema and instruct the model to populate it, then route records below a threshold to human review
Route a fixed random 10% of all records to humans regardless of the model's certainty
Send everything to humans first and let the model only double-check their work
Rely on request latency as a proxy: slower responses indicate lower confidence
rationale: A model-populated confidence field plus a threshold gives targeted, per-record escalation aligned with the business rule. Random sampling escalates the wrong records and misses genuinely uncertain ones. Human-first review discards the automation benefit. Latency does not correlate with correctness and is a meaningless confidence signal.
referenceText: Design structured outputs to include signals like confidence so uncertain results can be escalated for human review.

stem: You are building a production extraction service that must call tools, validate results, and retry on failure within a managed agent loop, and you want to build it in TypeScript or Python without hand-rolling the orchestration. Which foundation is the best fit?
options: Build on the Claude Agent SDK, which provides the agent loop, tool orchestration, and error handling primitives
Write a bespoke while-loop around raw HTTP calls and manage every retry and tool dispatch yourself
Use Claude Code interactively and copy each extraction result out by hand
Deploy a single-shot completion call with no loop and accept whatever comes back
rationale: The Agent SDK provides the harness (agent loop, tool use orchestration, error handling) so you can build a reliable extraction service without reimplementing that machinery. A hand-rolled loop reinvents what the SDK already provides and is error-prone. Interactive Claude Code is not a production service. A single-shot call has no retry or tool orchestration for a robust pipeline.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK provides the building blocks (the agent loop, tool use, and error handling) for building production agents.

stem: Several internal applications need to run the same document-extraction capability, and you want it reusable across different clients (a chat app, a batch job, an IDE assistant) without duplicating logic. Which Model Context Protocol construct best exposes it?
options: Implement the extraction capability as an MCP tool on a server so any MCP-compatible client can invoke it
Paste the extraction prompt into each application's codebase separately
Expose the documents as MCP resources and let each client write its own extraction prompt
Distribute the schema file over email so each team wires it up independently
rationale: MCP tools are the standard way to expose an executable capability that any MCP client can discover and call, giving reuse across apps. Copy-pasting the prompt duplicates and diverges logic. Exposing only the documents as resources still forces each client to reimplement extraction. Emailing a schema provides no shared runtime capability.
referenceText: Tools in MCP let servers expose executable functionality that clients can discover and invoke.

stem: You want your extraction workflow to run non-interactively in a CI job: read documents from a directory, produce JSON, and fail the build if validation fails. Which Claude Code capability enables this?
options: Run Claude Code in headless mode with the -p (print) flag so it executes non-interactively and emits output CI can consume
Keep a developer logged into an interactive Claude Code session during every CI run
Screen-scrape the interactive TUI output from the CI logs
Disable Claude Code and have CI email a human to run the extraction manually
rationale: Headless mode (claude -p) is designed for scripted, non-interactive automation such as CI pipelines, producing output a build can act on. Requiring an interactive human session defeats automation. Scraping TUI output is brittle and unsupported. Emailing a human removes the automation entirely.
referenceText: Use headless mode (the -p flag) to run Claude Code non-interactively in scripts, CI, and other automation.

stem: Your extraction repo has a canonical schema at schemas/invoice.json, a required validation command, and a rule that new fields must be nullable. You want Claude Code to follow these conventions automatically on every task in the repo. Where should you record them?
options: In a CLAUDE.md file at the repo root so Claude Code loads the conventions as project memory on each session
In a comment at the top of one random source file and hope Claude reads it
In the pull request template so reviewers can remind Claude after the fact
In a personal note kept outside the repository
rationale: CLAUDE.md is the documented project-memory file that Claude Code loads automatically, making it the right home for schema location and extraction conventions. A comment in an arbitrary file is not reliably discovered. A PR template only helps humans after work is done. A note outside the repo is never seen by Claude Code.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files are automatically loaded into context and are the place to record project conventions and commands.

stem: You force a single extraction tool, but on some documents the model returns two parallel tool_use blocks (partial records), breaking your one-record-per-document assumption. Which request setting most directly prevents multiple simultaneous tool calls?
options: Set disable_parallel_tool_use to true in tool_choice so the model emits at most one tool call per turn
Lower max_tokens so there is only room for one tool_use block
Add a stop_sequence after the first tool call to cut off the second
Increase temperature so the model commits to one interpretation
rationale: disable_parallel_tool_use in tool_choice constrains the model to a single tool call per turn, directly enforcing one record per document. Shrinking max_tokens risks truncating even the first record. A stop_sequence cannot reliably target a second tool_use block. Higher temperature increases variability rather than constraining call count.
referenceText: Set disable_parallel_tool_use to true to prevent Claude from calling multiple tools in a single response.

stem: The model keeps inventing values for fields that are simply not present in some documents. Which two changes most directly reduce these hallucinated fields? (Select 2.)
options: Explicitly instruct the model to return null (or 'not found') for any field whose value is absent from the document
Provide clear, specific descriptions for each schema field so the model knows exactly what to look for
Raise max_tokens so the model has more room to fill in every field
Add few-shot examples that only ever show fully populated documents
rationale: Giving the model an explicit way to signal absence (null / 'not found') removes the pressure to fabricate, and precise field descriptions reduce misattribution. More max_tokens does nothing about hallucination and can encourage the model to keep filling. Examples that are always fully populated teach the model that every field should be present, increasing fabrication on sparse documents.
referenceText: To reduce hallucinations, give Claude an out to say a value is not found, and describe exactly what each field should contain.

stem: Your extraction pipeline has multiple stages (classify document type, extract fields, cross-check totals). Between stages you currently pass the model's free-form prose, and later stages misparse it. What is the most reliable way to carry state between stages?
options: Persist each stage's output as a validated structured JSON state object that the next stage consumes programmatically
Concatenate each stage's full natural-language response and pass it forward as one growing string
Re-run the entire extraction from scratch at every stage to avoid passing state
Store only the final stage's output and reconstruct earlier state by guessing
rationale: Passing validated structured JSON state between stages makes inter-stage handoffs machine-parseable and reliable, avoiding the ambiguity of prose. Accumulating free-form text is exactly what is breaking parsing. Re-running everything each stage wastes cost and discards accumulated results. Reconstructing earlier state by guessing loses information and introduces errors.
referenceText: Maintain state between steps as structured data rather than free-form text so each step can reliably consume the previous one's output.
